title: Universal Evasion Attacks on Summarization Scoring
human_text: The primary reason is that NLU is clearly required to characterize summary quality, e.g., semantic similarity to determine adequacy (Morris, 2020) , or textual entailment (Dagan et al., 2006) to determine fidelity. Besides, summary scoring is similar to automated essay scoring (AES), which is a 50-year-old task measuring grammaticality, cohesion, relevance etc. of written texts (Ke and Ng, 2019) . Moreover, recent advances in automatic scoring also support this argument well. Automatic scoring is gradually transitioning from well-established metrics measuring N-gram overlap (BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002) , ROUGE (Lin, 2004) , METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) , etc.) to emerging metrics aimed at computing semantic similarity through pre-trained neural models (BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2019b) , MoverScore (Zhao et al., 2019) , BLEURT (Sellam et al., 2020) , etc.) These emerging scores exhibit two characteristics that stand-alone machine learning systems typically have: one is that some can be fine-tuned for human cognition; the other is that they still have room to improve and still have to learn how to match human ratings.
paraphrased_text: One main reason for the necessity of Natural Language Understanding (NLU) in assessing summary quality is its role in determining adequacy through semantic similarity and fidelity through textual entailment. Summary scoring, akin to Automated Essay Scoring (AES), has been a long-standing practice evaluating aspects like grammaticality, cohesion, and relevance in written texts. Recent advancements in automatic scoring further support the integration of NLU in this domain. While traditional metrics such as BLEU, ROUGE, and METEOR have been widely used for measuring N-gram overlap, newer metrics like BERTScore, MoverScore, and BLEURT are focusing on computing semantic similarity using pre-trained neural models. These evolving scores possess characteristics that align with standalone machine learning systems, as some can be fine-tuned for human understanding and they continue to evolve to better match human ratings.

title: Multilingual Generative Language Models for Zero-Shot Cross-Lingual Event Argument Extraction
human_text: Several works have demonstrated the great success of generation-based models on monolingual structured prediction tasks, including named entity recognition (Yan et al., 2021) , relation extraction (Huang et al., 2021b; Paolini et al., 2021) , and event extraction (Du et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021; Hsu et al., 2021; Lu et al., 2021 ). Yet, as mentioned in Section 1, their designed generating targets are language-dependent. Accordingly, directly applying their methods to the zero-shot cross-lingual setting would result in less-preferred performance. There are growing interests recently to incorporate prompts on pre-trained language models in order to guide the models' behavior or elicit knowledge (Peng et al., 2019; Sheng et al., 2020; Shin et al., 2020; Schick and Schütze, 2021; Qin and Eisner, 2021; Scao and Rush, 2021) .
paraphrased_text: Generation-based models have shown significant success in monolingual structured prediction tasks like named entity recognition, relation extraction, and event extraction. However, these models are designed with language-dependent generating targets, as discussed in Section 1. When applied directly to zero-shot cross-lingual scenarios, their performance may be suboptimal. To address this issue, there is a growing interest in incorporating prompts into pre-trained language models to guide their behavior and extract knowledge effectively.

title: IndicNLG Benchmark: Multilingual Datasets for Diverse NLG Tasks in Indic Languages
human_text: IndicNLG Benchmark complements the IndicGLUE benchmark (Kakwani et al., 2020) for Indic natural language understanding (NLU). NLG Tasks IndicNLG Benchmark focuses on data-to-text generation (Reiter and Dale, 1997; Gatt and Krahmer, 2018; Lebret et al., 2016) , headline generation (Banko et al., 2000) , sentence summarization (Rush et al., 2015) , paraphrasing (McKeown, 1979; Meteer and Shaked, 1988; Madnani and Dorr, 2010) and question generation (Brown et al., 2005; Ali et al., 2010; Heilman, 2011; Du et al., 2017) . Following previous works, we create datasets for each task automatically from structured data such as Wikipedia (Lebret et al., 2016) or news articles (Banko et al., 2000) or by leveraging parallel corpora (Madnani and Dorr, 2010) or by machine translation of an English dataset. Furthermore, we clean the datasets using regular expression and statistics based cleaning.
paraphrased_text: The IndicNLG Benchmark, designed to complement the IndicGLUE benchmark for Indic natural language understanding (NLU), focuses on various NLG tasks such as data-to-text generation, headline generation, sentence summarization, paraphrasing, and question generation. Drawing inspiration from prior research, we have automated the creation of datasets for each task by extracting information from structured sources like Wikipedia or news articles, utilizing parallel corpora, or translating English datasets. To ensure data quality, we meticulously clean the datasets using a combination of regular expression and statistical methods.

title: Fine-mixing: Mitigating Backdoors in Fine-tuned Language Models
human_text: Backdoor attacks have been conducted on different DNN architectures, including CNNs (Gu et al., 2019; Dumford and Scheirer, 2018) , LSTMs (Dai et al., 2019) , and fine-tuned language models (Kurita et al., 2020 ). In the meantime, a body of work has been proposed to alleviate backdoor attacks, which can be roughly categorized into backdoor detection methods (Huang et al., 2020; Harikumar et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020; Erichson et al., 2020; Kwon, 2020; Chen et al., 2018) and backdoor mitigation methods (Yao et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018a; Zhao et al., 2020a; Li et al., 2021c,b) . Most of these works were conducted in CV to defend image models. Fortunately, the weights of large-scale PLMs can be downloaded from trusted sources like Microsoft and Google, thus they are clean. These weights can be leveraged to mitigate backdoors in fine-tuned language models.
paraphrased_text: Different types of deep neural network (DNN) architectures, such as Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Long Short-Term Memory networks (LSTMs), and fine-tuned language models, have been targeted by backdoor attacks. To address this issue, various approaches have been developed, which can be broadly classified into backdoor detection methods and backdoor mitigation methods. While most of the research has focused on defending image models in computer vision (CV), it is worth noting that the weights of large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs) from reputable sources like Microsoft and Google are considered safe and can be utilized to counter backdoor attacks in fine-tuned language models.

title: Kronecker Decomposition for {GPT} Compression
human_text: Although Transformer-based PLMs are powerful in performance, their huge size is a barrier for efficient training or inference of these models on lower capacity devices with memory, computation and energy constraints. Therefore, there has been a growing volume of literature focused on developing frameworks for compressing these large PLMs. Like other deep learning models, the main directions of model compression for PLMs are using following methods in isolation or combination: low-bit quantization (Gong et al., 2014; Prato et al., 2019) , pruning (Han et al., 2015) , knowledge distillation (KD) (Hinton et al., 2015) and matrix decomposition (Yu et al., 2017; Lioutas et al., 2020) . PLMs can be divided into encoder-based and auto-regressive models such as the BERT (Devlin et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019) and GPT (Brown et al., 2020) family respectively. Although the size of BERT family models is usually smaller than the GPT family, compressing the BERT family has been investigated much more in the literature (e.g. DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019) , TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2019) , MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020) , ALP-KD (Passban et al., 2021) , MATE-KD (Rashid et al., 2021) , Annealing-KD (Jafari et al., 2021) and BERTQuant (Zhang et al., 2020) ).
paraphrased_text: While Transformer-based Pre-trained Language Models (PLMs) are known for their high performance, their large size poses challenges for training or using them on devices with limited memory, computation, and energy resources. Consequently, there is a growing body of research dedicated to developing methods to compress these large PLMs. Similar to other deep learning models, common approaches for compressing PLMs include low-bit quantization, pruning, knowledge distillation (KD), and matrix decomposition. PLMs can be categorized into encoder-based models like BERT and auto-regressive models like GPT. Although BERT models are typically smaller than GPT models, there has been more focus on compressing BERT models in the literature. Examples of compressed BERT models include DistilBERT, TinyBERT, MobileBERT, ALP-KD, MATE-KD, Annealing-KD, and BERTQuant.

title: {T}eg{T}ok: Augmenting Text Generation via Task-specific and Open-world Knowledge
human_text: As knowledge can help to understand the input text and its surrounding context, many previous studies explored the leverage of knowledge bases (Speer and Havasi, 2012; Speer et al., 2017; Koncel-Kedziorski et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2021) or unstructured texts (Zhang et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018c; Dinan et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020b) for the text generation task, and they have demonstrated promising performance on generating informative and coherent texts. To incorporate unstructured knowledge from the web, retrieval-augmented text generation (Lewis et al., 2020b) has been widely explored. Besides, researchers also introduced the paradigm of retrieve-and-edit (Hashimoto et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Ren et al., 2020) or exemplar-based decoding (Peng et al., 2019; Gupta et al., 2020) to enhance the generation processes with similar input-output pairs come from the specific task. More related works about knowledge-enhanced text generation can be referred to Yu et al. (2020b) .
paraphrased_text: Previous studies have explored the use of knowledge bases and unstructured texts to improve text generation. By leveraging knowledge, researchers have achieved promising results in generating informative and coherent texts. Techniques such as retrieval-augmented text generation and retrieve-and-edit have been widely studied to incorporate knowledge from the web. Additionally, exemplar-based decoding has been introduced to enhance generation processes using input-output pairs specific to the task. For more information on knowledge-enhanced text generation, refer to Yu et al. (2020b).

title: Are Abstractive Summarization Models truly 'Abstractive'? An Empirical Study to Compare the two Forms of Summarization
human_text: More recently, neural network-based extractive summarization have gained more popularity (Alami et al., 2019; There has also been significant work in abstractive summarization (Genest and Lapalme, 2012; Barzilay et al., 1999; Tanaka et al., 2009) . These methods produce summaries using words that are not present in the source text and hence these methods have gained more popularity over the last few years. Transformer models with selfsupervised training (Devlin et al., 2018; Radford et al., 2018; Raffel et al., 2019a; Yang et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2019) have shown to perform well on language learning when fine-tuned on various NLP tasks. More recently, BART (Lewis et al., 2019) , PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2019a) and T5 (Raffel et al., 2019b) have shown state-of-the-art performance in abstractive summarization, so we analyze the generated summaries of these models in comparison with SOTA methods in extractive summarization (Zhong et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2019) .
paraphrased_text: In recent years, neural network-based extractive summarization has become increasingly popular, with significant advancements also seen in abstractive summarization methods. Abstractive summarization techniques generate summaries using words not directly from the source text, leading to their growing popularity. Transformer models trained with self-supervision have demonstrated strong performance in language tasks when fine-tuned for various NLP applications. Recent models like BART, PEGASUS, and T5 have shown state-of-the-art results in abstractive summarization, prompting a comparison of their output with leading methods in extractive summarization.

title: Grafting Pre-trained Models for Multimodal Headline Generation
human_text: The task is closely relevant to text summarization (Zhang et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2022) while it is much tougher because redundancy and complementary between multiple modalities should be studied (Jangra et al., 2021) . Many literatures have been based on pre-extracted unimodal sequential representation and cross attention mechanism to obtain the fused feature (Li et al., 2020d; Khullar and Arora, 2020; Besides, some researchers took efforts to sufficiently fusing the multimedia inputs by hierarchical fusion (Liu et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021a) . The objective of modality consistency is another tool to guide the learning of multimodal fusion (Zhu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021b) . In this paper, we propose a grafting video-text generation model and a novel joint-modality layer which is designed to capture the commonality and specialty of multiple modalities.
paraphrased_text: The task at hand revolves around text summarization and presents a significant challenge due to the need to address redundancy and complementarity among multiple modalities. Various studies have focused on utilizing pre-extracted unimodal sequential representations and cross-attention mechanisms to integrate features effectively. Additionally, researchers have explored hierarchical fusion techniques to combine multimedia inputs comprehensively. Ensuring modality consistency is crucial for guiding the multimodal fusion process. In this study, we introduce a novel video-text generation model that incorporates a joint-modality layer aimed at capturing both the shared characteristics and unique aspects of different modalities.

title: Aligning Recommendation and Conversation via Dual Imitation
human_text: Conversational recommendation systems (CRS) aim to obtain user interests through conversational interaction and make persuasive recommendations (Jannach et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Ren et al., 2021) . To track the user interest shift in conversation, an intuitive strategy is to ask related questions (Kostric et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022b) which leads to question-based CRS (Lei et al., 2020; Deng et al., 2021) . Limited by predefined templates for asking and recommending, it is difficult for question-based CRS to flexibly adopt different contexts and converse in a human-like manner. Towards more flexible conversation, generationbased CRS (Li et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2021) capture user interests from context and generate responses containing persuasive explanations for recommendation. Limited by sparse context and language complexity (Lu et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2022) , it is challenging for generation-based CRS to track user interest shift in conversation.
paraphrased_text: Conversational recommendation systems (CRS) aim to understand user interests through interactive conversations and provide persuasive recommendations by asking questions and generating responses. Question-based CRS relies on asking related questions during the conversation to track the user's interests, while generation-based CRS captures user interests from the context and generates responses with persuasive explanations for recommendations. However, question-based CRS is limited by predefined templates, making it challenging to adapt to different contexts and converse in a natural way. On the other hand, generation-based CRS faces difficulties in tracking user interest changes due to sparse context and language complexity.

title: A Snapshot into the Possibility of Video Game Machine Translation
human_text: In many cases, even though the game might have been translated and it is playable, fans fail to be impressed because the poor quality of the localisation defeats its own purpose: to thrill and engage the gamer. Language and culture are ever-present elements in us and the things we do, players cannot help but notice continuous serious mistakes in the game, and it will erode their trust in some developer and publisher brands. But what makes video game translation such an arduous task and how could machines play a role in it if it already proves challenging for humans? In addition to the common pitfalls of translation and creative works, translators in the field are typically presented with spreadsheets in which text segments are not ordered chronologically and do not offer any other kind of contextual information, concerning for instance the communication medium, the type of discourse, 1 As the Web gave rise to the development of a "user-participatory culture" (Jenkins, 2006) and "user-generated content" (van Dijck, 2009) , the translation sector similarly witnessed the emergence of "user" or "amateur" crowdsourced translations (Lavault-Olléon, 2011; Gambier, 2016; Doherty, 2016) .
paraphrased_text: In many cases, fans are not impressed with translated games that are playable but lack quality localization. This defeats the purpose of engaging and thrilling gamers. Language and culture are integral to our experiences, so players easily notice serious mistakes in games, which can damage their trust in developers and publishers. Why is video game translation so challenging, and can machines assist where humans struggle? Translators often face disorganized spreadsheets lacking contextual information, such as communication medium or type of discourse. The rise of user-participatory culture on the Web has led to amateur crowdsourced translations in the translation sector.

title: Summarizing Dialogues with Negative Cues
human_text: Unlike the field of well-organized text merely from a single person, dialogues often consist of utterances coming from two or more interlocutors, where the conversations are often informal, verbose and repetitive, sprinkled with false-starts, backchannels, reconfirmations, hesitations, speaker interruptions (Sacks et al., 1978) and the key information is often scattered throughout the whole chat. The above properties of conversations make it difficult to concentrate on the scattered salient utterances. Recent studies incorporate intrinsic information of dialogues to handle the challenges for summarizing dialogues, such as topic features (Liu et al., 2019b; Li et al., 2019; Chen and Yang, 2020; Liu et al., 2021a) , dialogue acts (Goo and Chen, 2018), conversation stages (Chen and Yang, 2020) and coreference information (Liu et al., 2021b) . The main idea of such existing summarization systems is to directly learn the salient information of the input dialogues with various architecture designed or extra knowledge added. Differently, this work proposes to train a dialogue summarization system by explicitly telling the model the unimportant/redundant pieces of an input dialogue, so that the model is able to focus less on the given negative hints and pay more attention to the salient information.
paraphrased_text: In contrast to well-organized written text created by a single author, dialogues typically involve exchanges between multiple speakers. These conversations are often casual and can include interruptions, hesitations, repetitions, and other informal elements that make it challenging to extract key information. Recent research has addressed this challenge by considering various aspects of dialogues, such as topic features, dialogue acts, conversation stages, and coreference information. Existing dialogue summarization systems aim to identify important information through specialized architectures or additional knowledge. In this study, we propose a different approach: training a dialogue summarization system to recognize unimportant or redundant elements in a dialogue, allowing the model to focus on the most relevant information.

title: Practice Makes a Solver Perfect: Data Augmentation for Math Word Problem Solvers
human_text: Other frequently used methods include inducing spelling mistakes (Belinkov and Bisk, 2018) , synonym replacement (Zhang et al., 2016) , identifying close embeddings from a defined search space (Alzantot et al., 2018) , round trip translations (Sennrich et al., 2016) , paraphrasing techniques (Kumar et al., 2019) and words predicted by language model (Kobayashi, 2018) among many others. These methods are specific to the task at hand and needs to be adapted such that the generated augmentations bring diversity in the concerned dataset. Proposed Augmentation Approach: Data augmentation generates new data by modifying existing data points through transformations based on prior knowledge about the problem domain. We introduce carefully selected transformations on well known text augmentation techniques to develop examples suited for the task of MWP. These transformations help in increasing the diversity and size of problem set in existing datasets.
paraphrased_text: Some commonly used methods for data augmentation in text include introducing spelling errors, replacing words with synonyms, finding similar words from a specified search space, translating text back and forth, using paraphrasing techniques, and selecting words predicted by a language model. These methods are tailored to the specific task and should be adjusted to ensure that the generated variations enhance the diversity of the dataset. Our proposed approach for data augmentation involves applying selected transformations to well-established text augmentation techniques to create examples suitable for the MWP task. These transformations aim to boost the diversity and scale of the problem set within existing datasets.

title: Making a Semantic Event-type Ontology Multilingual
human_text: Similarly, efforts to link data have been traceable for years, such as the Multilingual Central Repository (MCR), a cross-lingual framework for developing Wordnets (Guinovart et al., 2021) integrating information from Wordnets and ontologies (Atserias et al., 2004) . The Event and Situation Ontology (ESO) reuses and maps across existing resources and also extracts information from text (Segers et al., 2016) . Another lexical-semantic resource called Uby gathers information from English and German resources (Gurevych et al., 2012; Eckle-Kohler et al., 2015) . In terms of Linked Data, there have been previous projects aiming to integrate knowledge kept in the individual resources, especially projects for FrameNet (Ide, 2014) and Word-Net (McCrae et al., 2014) . We are aware of several projects very close to SynSem-Class in that they are also integrating various existing resources for verbs in a common framework, allowing interoperability among all these sources.
paraphrased_text: Efforts to link data have been ongoing for years. For example, the Multilingual Central Repository (MCR) is a cross-lingual framework that integrates information from Wordnets and ontologies. The Event and Situation Ontology (ESO) reuses and maps across existing resources, extracting information from text. Another resource, Uby, gathers lexical-semantic information from English and German resources. Previous projects have aimed to integrate knowledge from individual resources, particularly for FrameNet and WordNet. Projects like SynSem-Class are also integrating various existing resources for verbs in a common framework to allow interoperability among these sources.

title: Argument Novelty and Validity Assessment via Multitask and Transfer Learning
human_text: Additionally, one might desire specific criteria in a generated conclusion, like being informative (Syed et al., 2021) . In this regard, the SharedTask at the 9th Workshop on Argument Mining proposed two quality dimensions of argument conclusions to be assessed. The first is validity, defined as whether a given conclusion can be logically inferred from its premises. The second is novelty, assessing whether the conclusion goes beyond what is mentioned in the premises to provide novel insights.
paraphrased_text: Moreover, individuals may seek specific attributes in a generated conclusion, such as being informative (Syed et al., 2021). To address this, the SharedTask at the 9th Workshop on Argument Mining introduced two key quality dimensions for evaluating argument conclusions. The first dimension is validity, which determines if a conclusion can be logically derived from its premises. The second dimension is novelty, which evaluates if the conclusion offers new perspectives beyond the information presented in the premises.

title: Contextualized language models for semantic change detection: lessons learned
human_text: Dedicated workshops on computational approaches to historical language change took place at the ACL conferences (Tahmasebi et al., 2019 (Tahmasebi et al., , 2021b (Tahmasebi et al., , 2022) ) and the results of the SemEval-2020 Task 1 on unsupervised lexical semantic change detection were announced in March 2020 (Schlechtweg et al., 2020) . Shared tasks for other languages followed soon (Basile et al., 2020; Kutuzov and Pivovarova, 2021) . The majority of the SemEval-2020 shared task participants employed methods based on word embeddings of various types. About half of them tried to make use of contextualized ('token-based') architectures like ELMo (Peters et al., 2018a) or BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) . Although the winning systems still used non-contextualized ('static' or 'type-based') embeddings like word2vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) , the difference in scores was not dramatic and we are most likely going to see more work in this direction.
paraphrased_text: Specialized workshops focusing on computational approaches to historical language change were held at the ACL conferences, with the outcomes of the SemEval-2020 Task 1 on unsupervised lexical semantic change detection being unveiled in March 2020. Subsequent shared tasks for different languages were introduced shortly after. The majority of participants in the SemEval-2020 shared task leveraged various types of word embeddings, with approximately half of them exploring contextualized ('token-based') architectures such as ELMo and BERT. While the winning systems still utilized non-contextualized ('static' or 'type-based') embeddings like word2vec, the difference in performance scores was not significant, indicating a potential shift towards more exploration in this area.

title: Enhancing Documentation of {H}upa with Automatic Speech Recognition
human_text: Accordingly, it becomes essential to have adequate transcriptions of recordings for users to find the information they are interested in. Transcribing recordings, however, is also an extremely time-consuming endeavor, leading to what is sometimes called the "transcription bottleneck" (Gupta and Boulianne, 2020; Zahrer et al., 2020; Ćavar et al., 2016; Shi et al., 2021) , which refers to the situation where the language data is mostly in the form of (archival) recordings, and transcriptions of the data are not yet available. Hupa (ISO 639-3 code: hup; Glottolog code: hupa1240), a critically endangered Native American language of northwestern California, provides a case in point. Since the early 2000s, Mrs. Verdena Parker, an elder from the Hoopa Valley Tribe, has generously shared her knowledge of the language with other community members and academic researchers. Recordings produced by and with Mrs. Parker include several hours of monolingual Hupa narratives and other texts, as well as over 800 hours of linguistic interviews that are a mixture of Hupa and English as the elicitation metalanguage.
paraphrased_text: Therefore, it is crucial to have accurate transcriptions of recordings so that users can easily access the information they are seeking. However, transcribing recordings is a time-consuming task, leading to what is known as the "transcription bottleneck." This term refers to a situation where language data primarily exists in recorded form, yet transcriptions are unavailable. An example of this challenge is seen in the case of Hupa, a critically endangered Native American language spoken in northwestern California. Since the early 2000s, Mrs. Verdena Parker, an elder from the Hoopa Valley Tribe, has shared her language knowledge through recordings, including monolingual Hupa narratives and linguistic interviews in both Hupa and English.

title: {LITE}: {I}ntent-based Task Representation Learning Using Weak Supervision
human_text: The goal of this work is to develop a single, general-purpose encoding system that converts todo task texts into real-valued vector representations (Fig. 1 ). Using one encoding system for multiple task functions (task detection, organization, recommendation, etc.) as opposed to having multiple dedicated encoders saves the computational costs of updating models regularly and encoding texts from millions of users. Representation learning has been extensively studied in natural language processing (Camacho-Collados and Pilehvar, 2018) . Adapting models pre-trained on massive amounts of raw texts to a target domain or a task has become common practice (Qiu et al., 2020) , with many publicly available pre-trained models such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) , GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2018) , and sentence encoders (Cer et al., 2018; Reimers and Gurevych, 2019) .
paraphrased_text: The aim of this work is to create a versatile encoding system that can convert user-generated to-do task texts into numerical vector representations (see Figure 1). By using a single encoding system for various task functions like task detection, organization, and recommendation, instead of multiple dedicated encoders, we can save on computational costs associated with model updates and text encoding for a large user base. Representation learning in natural language processing has been widely researched, and it is common practice to adapt pre-trained models from raw text data to specific domains or tasks. There are several publicly available pre-trained models, including BERT, GPT-2, and sentence encoders, that facilitate this process.

title: Do Vision-and-Language Transformers Learn Grounded Predicate-Noun Dependencies?
human_text: A model that does not track dependencies would judge the distractor sentence "A man is wearing glasses" as equally likely, as there is a man is the image, as well as a person that is wearing glasses. a better understanding of their exact multimodal knowledge. A range of studies has investigated their ability to map words to their visual referents for nouns (Kazemzadeh et al., 2014; Mao et al., 2016; Shekhar et al., 2017) and verbs (Ronchi and Perona, 2015; Yatskar et al., 2016; Pratt et al., 2020; Hendricks and Nematzadeh, 2021) , but there are only a few studies on whether recent V&L models can capture multimodal syntactic dependencies between words and concepts. In this paper, we explore how well V&L models learn predicate-noun dependencies across modalities (see example in Figure 1 ). To this end, we create an evaluation set that contains carefully selected images and pairs of sentences with minimal differences.
paraphrased_text: A model that does not consider dependencies would view the distractor sentence "A man is wearing glasses" as equally probable because there is both a man and a person wearing glasses in the image. While previous studies have looked into how well these models can associate words with visual objects for nouns and verbs, there is limited research on whether current Vision and Language (V&L) models can understand the syntactic relationships between words and concepts. This paper investigates how effectively V&L models learn connections between verbs and nouns across different types of information, as illustrated in Figure 1. To do this, we have created an evaluation dataset with carefully chosen images and sets of sentences that have minimal variations.

title: Full-Text Argumentation Mining on Scientific Publications
human_text: It is the smallest unit of argumentation, and may span anything from an in-sentence clause up to multiple full sentences. ADU recognition requires both detecting argumentative spans, as well as classifying them into predefined categories. Typically, this is realised as sequence tagging task similar to NER, where a sequence of tokens X = {t 1 , t 2 , ..., t N } is assigned with a corresponding N -length sequence of labels Y = {l 1 , l 2 , ..., l N } with l i ∈ C where C is the set of tags that result from converting the ADU types into a tagging scheme like BIO2. 2 In scholarly AM, common ADU classes are (Own / Background) Claim, and Evidence, Data, or Warrant (Green, 2014; Lauscher et al., 2018b).
paraphrased_text: The smallest unit of argumentation can range from a clause within a sentence to multiple full sentences. Identifying argumentative spans involves both detecting these spans and categorizing them into predefined groups. This is typically done by assigning labels to a sequence of tokens, similar to Named Entity Recognition (NER). In academic argumentation mining (AM), common ADU classes include Own/Background Claim, Evidence, Data, or Warrant.

title: Cross-lingual Approaches for the Detection of Adverse Drug Reactions in German from a Patient's Perspective
human_text: By using their own non-expert language, e.g. using laymen terms for describing their situation, they create a rich data source for pharmacovigilance from a patient's perspective in a wide variety of languages. Therefore, there is theoretically a huge amount of text to leverage. On the other side, however, this text is noisy (e.g. spelling mistakes), incomplete (e.g. deleted messages) and the use of laymen terms and abbreviations (e.g. "AD" as a generic name for any anti-depressant) complicates the processing of these data (Seiffe et al., 2020; Basaldella et al., 2020) . It is furthermore difficult to collect these resources (e.g. finding informative keywords to search Twitter) and researchers are soon confronted with privacy issues when handling social media data. Thus, only a small amount of (annotated) data is publicly available for research and unfortunately, most of these data are in English.
paraphrased_text: By using simple language that non-experts can understand to describe their experiences, patients contribute valuable data for pharmacovigilance from their perspective in various languages. This creates a vast amount of text that can be analyzed. However, this text often contains errors like spelling mistakes, is sometimes incomplete due to deleted messages, and includes layman terms and abbreviations that make data processing challenging. Obtaining these resources, such as finding relevant keywords on platforms like Twitter, can be difficult, and researchers must navigate privacy concerns when handling social media data. As a result, only a limited amount of publicly available (annotated) data is accessible for research, and most of it is in English.

title: On "Human Parity" and "Super Human Performance" in Machine Translation Evaluation
human_text: This sounds impressive, but should be discussed further and put into perspective. In this paper, we claim that the term "human parity" and, moreover, "super human performance" are misleading, in that they concern only very specific types of texts, for a very limited number of languages, in very specific conditions. Authors of course mention this when they discuss their results, see for example (Hassan et al., 2018) , but paper titles, and thereafter headlines in the press, leave apart these as details, making the general public confused about the real state of the art. Although our concerns are not new and have already been detailed by different authors, especially (Toral et al., 2018; Toral, 2019; Toral, 2020; Läubli et al., 2018; Läubli et al., 2020) , we think it is important to discuss them again, as these terms are themselves problematic.
paraphrased_text: This paper raises important points about the terms "human parity" and "super human performance" in the context of machine translation. While some recent studies claim to achieve super-human translation performance, it's crucial to consider that these results are based on specific types of texts, limited languages, and specific conditions. Authors often acknowledge these limitations in their discussions, but media headlines tend to overlook these nuances, leading to confusion among the general public about the true state of the art. While these concerns have been highlighted by various authors before, it's important to revisit and discuss them to address the problematic nature of these terms.

title: Drum Up SUPPORT: Systematic Analysis of Image-Schematic Conceptual Metaphors
human_text: ISCMs map these experiential, conceptual structures to the abstract domain. For instance, a person can physically lean on a concrete physical entity, e.g. a table, which entails the person pressing their body weight onto an entity that resists the push force. This physical experience can be mapped onto the abstract domain of emotional SUPPORT, such as in He leans on his friends in these trying times. Our approach relies on a series of steps to semi-automatically identify ISCMs in natural language: (a) detect whether a sequence is metaphoric or literal, (b) determine its constructional pattern, (c) identify its associated topics, and (d) identify its frames, from which we (e) derive underlying metaphoric projections. We extract sentences from the sample of The Coronavirus Corpus based on seed words related to "support".
paraphrased_text: Integrated Semantic Conceptual Metaphors (ISCMs) connect these experiential, conceptual structures to the abstract realm. For example, a person can physically lean on a tangible object, like a table, where they apply pressure with their body weight against a resisting force. This physical act can be linked to the abstract concept of emotional support, as seen in phrases like "He leans on his friends in these challenging times." Our methodology involves a series of steps to identify ISCMs in natural language semi-automatically: (a) distinguishing between metaphorical and literal sequences, (b) analyzing their constructional patterns, (c) pinpointing associated topics, (d) identifying frames, and (e) uncovering the underlying metaphoric projections. We select sentences from The Coronavirus Corpus sample using seed words related to "support."

title: {G}en{IE}: Generative Information Extraction

paraphrased_text: In this work, we address the task of closed information extraction (cIE), which focuses on extracting an exhaustive set of facts from natural language that adhere to predefined relation and entity constraints within a knowledge base (KB). Most existing methods tackle this problem using a pipeline approach. One approach involves extracting entity mentions and their relations from raw text as a starting point. We emphasize the significance of reporting performance based on the number of relation occurrences in the data. To support future research, we have made available pre-processed data, pre-trained models, and code in a user-friendly template on https://github.com/epfl-dlab/GenIE.

title: RL with KL penalties is better viewed as Bayesian inference
human_text: The optimal policy under the RL objective would be a minimalentropy LM generating a small set of sequences that obtain the highest reward. Then, we discuss how KL-regularised RL avoids distribution collapse due to its KL penalty. This constraint, we argue, transforms the problem from RL to Bayesian inference: updating a prior to conform with evidence provided by the reward. The Bayesian perspective moves KL-regularised RL closer to other divergence-minimisation-based approaches to fine-tuning LMs (Khalifa et al., 2021) and, more broadly, to other divergence-minimisation-based accounts of control (Levine, 2018; Hafner et al., 2020) . These divergence minimisation approaches naturally avoid the distribution collapse problem because they formalize the agent as a generative model.
paraphrased_text: The optimal strategy in Reinforcement Learning (RL) aims to minimize entropy in a Language Model (LM) to produce a concise set of sequences that yield maximum rewards. We then explore how KL-regularized RL mitigates distribution collapse through its KL penalty. This constraint shifts the focus from RL to Bayesian inference, where the model updates its prior based on reward-driven evidence. Viewing KL-regularized RL from a Bayesian standpoint aligns it with other divergence-minimization techniques for refining LMs (Khalifa et al., 2021) and broader control frameworks (Levine, 2018; Hafner et al., 2020). By treating the agent as a generative model, these divergence-minimization methods naturally circumvent distribution collapse issues.

title: MemPrompt: Memory-assisted Prompt Editing with User Feedback
human_text: In Tandon et al. (2022) , we show that using a memory of user feedback can be used to repair erroneous model in a supervised setting. In this work, we build upon the recent advances in few-shot prompting to modify GPT-3's behavior by adding user feedback to the query (prompt). Like others, we use GPT-3 with few-shot prompting, where the prompt consists of a prefix containing a few inputoutput "training" examples of the task, followed by the input x, e.g., a question, to operate on. However, while prior work has focused on constructing better prefixes, e.g., dynamically selecting good "training" examples based on the question (Le Scao and Rush, 2021; Liu et al., 2021a) , or even representing the prefix latently (Li and Liang, 2021) , our work elaborates the input x itself to clarify the intended task, by adding user feedback fb from previous misunderstandings. Similarly, our work can be seen as a form of retrieval-augmented QA.
paraphrased_text: In Tandon et al. (2022), we demonstrate that incorporating user feedback memory can help correct errors in a supervised learning scenario. Building on recent advancements in few-shot prompting, we enhance GPT-3's performance by integrating user feedback into the query (prompt). While previous studies have focused on improving the prefix of the prompt, our approach involves elaborating on the input itself to provide clarity on the task by incorporating user feedback from past misunderstandings. Our method can be viewed as a form of retrieval-augmented question answering.

title: The Multimodal Annotation Software Tool (MAST)
human_text: This tool, however, was designed to support single-user work and thus is not designed for remote, collaborative work. Furthermore, the annotations available for users are built-in, developed by the authors, and thus does not let users annotate documents using their own annotations. While these systems are effective in their intended uses, and many introduce insightful and useful tools, we find them to be limited in various ways. First, in order to offer users the most value, annotation tools should neither restrict researchers in the nature of the documents they can analyze, the annotations they can use, nor the shape of the regions they can define on the documents. In addition, because multimodal documents can be complex and function across many structures, annotation tools should include the ability to annotate the same regions across multiple dimensions.
paraphrased_text: This particular tool was specifically developed to cater to individual user needs and is not optimized for collaborative work in remote settings. Moreover, the built-in annotations provided are authored by the developers themselves, thus limiting users from incorporating their own annotations. While these systems offer effectiveness in their intended capacities and may introduce valuable features, they exhibit certain constraints. For instance, annotation tools should ideally offer users unrestricted flexibility in analyzing diverse types of documents, utilizing various annotations, and defining regions on documents. Furthermore, given the intricate nature of multimodal documents that can span across different structures, annotation tools should possess the capability to annotate identical regions across multiple dimensions.

title: UniK-QA: Unified Representations of Structured and Unstructured Knowledge for Open-Domain Question Answering
human_text: It is also difficult to handle questions that require reasoning with information from multiple sources. Having a more integrated system design that covers heterogeneous information sources has proven to be difficult. One main reason is that techniques used for KBQA and TextQA are drastically different. The former exploits the graph structure and/or semantic parsing to convert the question into a structured query, while TextQA has mostly settled on the retriever-reader architecture powered by pre-trained transformers. Recent work on multisource QA has tried to incorporate free text into graph nodes (Sun et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2019) to make texts amenable to KBQA methods, but the performance remains unconvincing.
paraphrased_text: Handling inquiries that necessitate the application of reasoning skills with data drawn from various sources poses a significant challenge. The development of a more cohesive system design encompassing diverse information sources has proven to be a complex endeavor. A key obstacle lies in the disparate methodologies employed in Knowledge Base Question Answering (KBQA) and Text Question Answering (TextQA). The former leverages graph structures and semantic parsing to transform queries into structured formats, whereas TextQA predominantly relies on the retriever-reader architecture supported by pretrained transformers. Recent studies in multisource Question Answering (QA) have endeavored to integrate unstructured text into graph nodes to align text data with KBQA techniques, as evidenced by the works of Sun et al. (2018) and Lu et al. (2019). Nevertheless, despite these efforts, the efficacy of such approaches remains inconclusive.

title: Self-supervised Representation Learning for Speech Processing
human_text: Representation learning is not an entirely new idea. This tutorial will briefly review what has been done before the wave of SSL in the speech community and the relations and differences between SSL and previous representation learning approaches. These approaches include clustering and mixture models (e.g., HMM, GMM) (Jansen and Church, 2011; Lee and Glass, 2012; Chung et al., 2013; Zhang and Glass, 2010) , and stacked representation learners (e.g., RBM, NAE, NCE, SparseCoding) (Mohamed and Hinton, 2010). Then, we discuss the design and implementation details of existing speech SSL approaches, which can be categorized into three types, Generative, Contrastive, and Predictive approaches. Generative approaches learn SSL representations by reconstructing input features given historical or unmasked ones.
paraphrased_text: Representation learning is a concept with a history that predates the recent surge of interest in semi-supervised learning within the speech community. This paper provides a succinct overview of the evolution of representation learning, examining its relationship with prior methodologies and highlighting the distinctions between semi-supervised learning (SSL) and earlier approaches. Previous techniques encompass clustering and mixture models such as Hidden Markov Models (HMM) and Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) (Jansen and Church, 2011; Lee and Glass, 2012; Chung et al., 2013; Zhang and Glass, 2010), as well as stacked representation learners like Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBM), Neural Autoencoders (NAE), Noise-Contrastive Estimation (NCE), and Sparse Coding (Mohamed and Hinton, 2010). Following this historical context, the paper examines the design and implementation specifics of existing speech semi-supervised learning approaches, which fall into three broad categories: Generative, Contrastive, and Predictive approaches. Generative methods involve learning SSL representations through the reconstruction of input features based on historical or unmasked data.

title: Toward Implicit Reference in Dialog: A Survey of Methods and Data

title: Zoom Out and Observe: News Environment Perception for Fake News Detection

Unfortunately, these potentially useful signals could be hardly considered by post-only and "zoom-in" methods, as they focus on digging in the direction towards inherent properties of a single post (e.g., styles, emotions and factual correctness), rather than observing the surrounding environments of the post. To enable fake news detection systems to exploit information from news environments, we propose the News Environment Perception Framework (NEP). As presented in Figure 3 , for the post p, we construct two news environments, MACROENV and MICROENV, using recent mainstream news data to facilitate the perception from different views.
paraphrased_text: In the world of micro news, the focus is mainly on the game itself, such as reporting on a player's shot, while additional information about Syria's unique celebration is provided to attract attention and increase the reach of this news item. However, traditional methods that concentrate solely on individual posts may overlook valuable signals from the broader news context. To address this issue and enhance fake news detection systems, we introduce the News Environment Perception Framework (NEP). In this framework, we create two news environments, MACROENV and MICROENV, based on current mainstream news data to allow for a more comprehensive understanding of each news post.

title: Metaphor Detection for Low Resource Languages: From Zero-Shot to Few-Shot Learning in Middle High German

A metaphor, as a semantic figure of speech, is a way of referring to one concept by mentioning another (Zymner, 2007) . An example for this would be the phrase the car drinks gasoline (Wilks, 1978) , where the word drinks from the domain of food consumption is applied to word car from the domains of transportation and machines. It carries over its base meaning of consumption of liquids, so that the reader understands that the car consumes fuel.

title: A Pipeline for Generating, Annotating and Employing Synthetic Data for Real World Question Answering
human_text: We release this annotation tool and its guidelines for general use. While we use and evaluate this pipeline in the domain of business news, the pipeline is sufficiently flexible to be applied to other domains, including potentially being applicable to abstractive QA. On benchmark datasets, such as the Corpus of Linguistic Acceptability (CoLA, Warstadt et al., 2019) which contains a wide range of examples from published linguistics literature, current state-of-the-art models (Sun et al., 2019) can achieve a Matthew's Correlation Coefficient score (Matthews, 1975) of approximately 0.775 (Wang et al., 2022) , exceeding human performance (0.713, Warstadt et al., 2019) in some cases, though this can vary significantly depending on the sentence's syntactic complexity and length (Warstadt and Bowman, 2020) .

title: Joint Alignment of Multi-Task Feature and Label Spaces for Emotion Cause Pair Extraction
human_text: To this end, we in this work propose a feature-task alignment (FTA) scheme of MTL for ECPE (cf. Figure 1(d) ), in which we explicitly split three parts of features, and align them to EE, CE and ECPE respectively. Meanwhile, aligning the label space in a MTL framework is crucial to overall ECPE performance, because intuitively all modules in the MTL process should reach a consensus. For example as in Figure 1 (a), once "c1" is recognized as a cause clause by ECPE module, it should not be further predicted as a non-cause clause by CE module. We notice that such label consistency is not guaranteed in existing MTL-based ECPE methods, which could inevitably hurt the prediction. Therefore we further introduce a inter-task alignment (ITA) mechanism (cf. 1(e)) that learns to pull closer the label distance between the ECPE and the combinations of EE&CE, ensuring label consistency.
paraphrased_text: In this study, we propose a Feature-Task Alignment (FTA) scheme for Multi-Task Learning (MTL) in Event-Centric Pattern Extraction (ECPE), as illustrated in Figure 1(d). The FTA scheme involves the explicit partitioning of features into three distinct parts, each aligned with Event Extraction (EE), Cause Extraction (CE), and ECPE tasks, respectively. Ensuring alignment of the label space within the MTL framework is critical for optimizing ECPE performance, as it is important for all modules in the MTL process to reach a consensus. For instance, as depicted in Figure 1(a), if "c1" is identified as a cause clause by the ECPE module, it should not subsequently be predicted as a non-cause clause by the CE module. It is observed that maintaining label consistency is not a guaranteed aspect in existing MTL-based ECPE methodologies, which could potentially compromise prediction accuracy. Consequently, we introduce an Inter-Task Alignment (ITA) mechanism, as shown in Figure 1(e), which aims to minimize the label discrepancy between ECPE and the combined tasks of EE and CE, thereby ensuring label consistency.

title: CogBERT: Cognition-Guided Pre-trained Language Models
human_text: The lower strands (including phonology, morphology, etc.) work together as the reader becomes accurate and automatic with repetition and practice. Meanwhile, the upper strands (including language structures, semantics, etc.) reinforce each other and weave together with the lower strands to produce a skilled reader. Inspired by previous work, we construct an initial cognitive feature set including 46 fine-grained cognitive features extracted from texts using the spaCy tool (Honnibal and Montani, 2017) and divide them into lower strands features (word-level) and upper strands features (semantic/syntax-level). However, not all cognitive features are statistically significant in eye-tracking data (Zuco 1.0 (Hollenstein et al., 2018) , Zuco 2.0 (Hollenstein et al., 2020) , and Geco (Cop et al., 2016 )). Hence, we filter out cognitive features that the human attention of words with these features is not significantly higher/lower than the average attention of words, retaining 14 usable cognitive features and group them as 8 independent features shown in Table 1 .
paraphrased_text: The different components of reading, such as phonology and morphology, work together as readers become more accurate and automatic through repetition and practice. Meanwhile, higher-level aspects like language structures and semantics support each other and combine with the lower-level components to create a proficient reader. Drawing on previous research, we created an initial set of 46 detailed cognitive features extracted from texts using the spaCy tool (Honnibal and Montani, 2017). These features were categorized into lower-level features (related to individual words) and upper-level features (related to meaning and syntax). However, not all of these cognitive features were found to be statistically significant in eye-tracking data from Zuco 1.0 (Hollenstein et al., 2018), Zuco 2.0 (Hollenstein et al., 2020), and Geco (Cop et al., 2016). Consequently, we filtered out cognitive features that did not result in significantly higher or lower human attention compared to the average attention given to words. This process left us with 14 useful cognitive features, which we grouped into 8 distinct features as shown in Table 1.

title: {R}otate{QVS}: Representing Temporal Information as Rotations in Quaternion Vector Space for Temporal Knowledge Graph Completion
human_text: Previous static KGC works (Sun et al., 2020; Schlichtkrull et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2020) learn explainable embeddings of various relation patterns, so that symmetric pattern (e.g. "co-author"), asymmetric pattern (e.g. "affiliation"), inverse pattern (e.g. "buyer" vs. "seller") and complex composition pattern (e.g. "father's wife (mother)" vs. "wife's father (father in law)") can be captured in static KGs. However, in TKGs, there are inherent connections between entities and their relations along with time-evolving. For example, the relation between Kit Harington and Rose Leslie is in love in 2012, becomes engaged in 2017, and then turns into married in 2018. To the best of our knowledge, very few of the existing TKGE methods can capture them. To address this problem, we take inspirations from Hamilton's quaternion number system (Hamilton, 1844; Zhang et al., 2019a; Gao et al., 2020) and propose a novel method based on quaternion.
paraphrased_text: Previous studies on static Knowledge Graph Completion (KGC) (Sun et al., 2020; Schlichtkrull et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2020) have focused on learning explainable embeddings representing various relation patterns in knowledge graphs. These patterns include symmetric relations (e.g., "co-author"), asymmetric relations (e.g., "affiliation"), inverse relations (e.g., "buyer" vs. "seller"), and complex compositional relations (e.g., "father's wife (mother)" vs. "wife's father (father-in-law)"). However, Temporal Knowledge Graphs (TKGs) introduce a new challenge as the connections between entities and relations evolve over time. For instance, the relationship between Kit Harington and Rose Leslie progresses from being "in love" in 2012, to "engaged" in 2017, and finally to "married" in 2018. Existing Temporal Knowledge Graph Embedding (TKGE) methods are limited in capturing such temporal dynamics. To address this gap, we draw inspiration from Hamilton's quaternion number system (Hamilton, 1844; Zhang et al., 2019a; Gao et al., 2020) and introduce a novel method based on quaternions.

title: RaP: Redundancy-aware Video-language Pre-training for Text-Video Retrieval
human_text: So we use the minimum dis-similarity between a patch and all tokens as its visual redundancy. Symmetrically, the redundancy of a token depends on how well it corresponds to the patches in the video, and we use the minimum dis-similarity between a token and all patches as its textual redundancy. To reduce the impact of high-redundant patches on learning text embedding or tokens on learning video embedding, we then propose redundancyaware contrastive learning. We take patch-text pairs as additional positives in video-to-text constrastive learning and assign smaller weights to pairs with high-redundant patches. paraphrased_text: The concept of visual and textual redundancy plays a crucial role in our approach. Visual redundancy is determined by calculating the minimum dissimilarity between a patch and all tokens, signifying the extent to which a patch is visually similar to the tokens. Conversely, textual redundancy is evaluated by measuring the minimum dissimilarity between a token and all patches, indicating the degree to which a token corresponds to the visual content in the video. To mitigate the influence of highly redundant patches on text embedding learning and redundant tokens on video embedding learning, we introduce a novel method called redundancy-aware contrastive learning. This approach involves incorporating patch-text pairs as additional positive samples in video-to-text contrastive learning, with reduced weights assigned to pairs containing high-redundancy patches. Likewise, in text-to-video contrastive learning, we include token-video pairs as additional positives, while assigning lower weights to pairs featuring highly redundant tokens.

title: Text Editing as Imitation Game
human_text: As a major obstacle, the training is on state-action demonstrations, where the encoding of the states and actions can be very different (Gu et al., 2018) . For instance, the mismatch of the lengths dimension between the state and action makes it tricky to implement for an auto-regressive modeling that benefits from a single, uniform representation. To tackle the issues above, we reformulate text editing as an imitation game controlled by a Markov Decision Process (MDP). To begin with, we define the input sequence as the initial state, the required operations as action sequences, and the output target sequence as the goal state. A learning agent needs to imitate an expert policy, respond to seen states with actions, and interact with the environment until the success of the eventual editing.
paraphrased_text: A significant challenge in training for natural language processing (NLP) tasks lies in handling state-action demonstrations, which may involve encoding states and actions that differ greatly (Gu et al., 2018). One particular issue is the disparity in dimensionality between the state and action representations, complicating the implementation of auto-regressive models that rely on a consistent, unified representation. To address these challenges, we propose a new approach that reframes text editing as an imitation game governed by a Markov Decision Process (MDP). In this framework, the input sequence serves as the initial state, the required operations are represented as action sequences, and the target output sequence corresponds to the goal state. An intelligent learning agent is then trained to mimic an expert policy, selecting actions in response to observed states and interacting with the environment until the desired editing outcome is achieved.

The proposed approach is partially related to some prior studies of hardness measurement in training deep learning models (Lin et al., 2017; Kalantidis et al., 2020; Hao et al., 2021) , whose focus is to guide the model to pay special attention to hard examples and prevent the vast number of easy negatives from overwhelming the training process. human_text: With thousands of students in MOOCs and hundreds of students in university classes, instructors increasingly apply the approach of peer-to-peer feedback (or, 'peer feedback'), where students provide feedback to their peers (van Popta et al., 2017; Lipnevich and Smith, 2018) . Peer-feedback enables instructors to provide individual feedback on every piece of coursework by leveraging the potential of students to provide feedback to each other (Piech et al., 2013) . Nevertheless, since students are often not experts in providing feedback, the instructors need to ensure that the feedback is helpful (Strijbos et al., 2010) . Research has shown that one factor determining feedback helpfulness is whether the feedback points are generic or specific (Lipnevich and Smith, 2018; Shute, 2008; Hattie and Timperley, 2007) .
paraphrased_text: With the increasing popularity of peer-to-peer feedback in both MOOCs with thousands of students and university classes with hundreds of students, instructors are turning to this approach as a way to provide individual feedback on student coursework. Peer feedback involves students giving feedback to their peers, allowing instructors to leverage the students' ability to provide feedback to each other. However, as students may not be experts in giving feedback, it is crucial for instructors to ensure that the feedback is helpful. Research has highlighted the importance of specificity in feedback, as opposed to generic feedback, in determining its helpfulness.

title: Consultation Checklists: Standardising the Human Evaluation of Medical Note Generation
human_text: We also show that correlation with human judgements increases when using CCs instead of the original clinician note as the reference for automatic evaluation metrics. There are a number of different approaches to quantitative human evaluation in NLG. Rating or Likert scales work well with few criteria, but lack explanatory power and fail to capture text quality (Hastie and Belz, 2014) . Adding more criteria partly resolves this, but at the cost of the evaluation task becoming more difficult and subjective (van der Lee et al., 2021) . For example, Moen et al. (2016) use a 30 item rating scale and report that subjects found it too difficult to use.
paraphrased_text: In our study, we found that correlation with human judgments significantly improves when using Crowd-Consensus Annotations (CCs) instead of the original clinician note as the reference for automatic evaluation metrics. In the field of Natural Language Generation (NLG), various methods have been explored for quantitatively evaluating human judgments. While Rating or Likert scales are effective for assessing a few criteria, they often lack detailed explanations and may not fully capture text quality (Hastie and Belz, 2014). Increasing the number of criteria can help address this limitation, but it can also make the evaluation task more challenging and subjective (van der Lee et al., 2021). For instance, Moen et al. (2016) utilized a 30-item rating scale and found that participants struggled with its complexity.

title: KOJAK: A New Corpus for Studying German Discourse Particle ja
human_text: Thus, the reason that the state of affairs is unquestionable is that the hearer already believes it (or has in the past). This is echoed by Döring and Repp (2016) : "ja is generally taken to indicate (roughly), that the speaker assumes that the proposition ja scopes over is already part of the common ground, i.e. that it is not new (see many of the references above)". We see that German discourse particle ja interacts crucially with how the discourse participants manage the common ground. However, most languages do not have such discourse particles. For example, English speakers may turn to phrases like you know or of course in the absence of such a particle.

This notion is supported by Döring and Repp (2016), who suggest that "ja" typically signals that the speaker assumes the proposition it accompanies is already established in the shared knowledge of the interlocutors, thus not introducing new information. The German discourse particle "ja" plays a pivotal role in shaping how participants in a conversation navigate the common ground. In contrast, many languages lack similar discourse particles. For instance, English speakers may utilize expressions such as "you know" or "of course" when such a particle is absent.

This approach to dealing with gestural elements in SLs stems from fact that SL researchers "naturally adopted the theoretical and analytic tools that were established in spoken language linguistics" (Lepic, 2019, p. 3) . Using these tools on SL data has led them to establish strict claims about lexicalization and grammaticalization of certain elements in some SLs (i.e., multiword expressions and morphologically complex signs (Lepic, 2019) ). human_text: CLIP is an imagetext matching system with two components, a text encoder (CLIP-T), and an image encoder (CLIP-ViT), which learn to encode paired inputs to closer output embeddings via contrastive loss. The trained representation has the following properties: cos(H j , V i )(i ̸ = j) (3) where H i is the CLIP text encoder output of X i , and V i is the CLIP image encoder output of Y i . The text-image input (X i , Y i ) is paired, and every (X j , Y k ) (j ̸ = k) is a non-pair. Since H i and V i are normalized and have a length of 1, H i can be used to approximate V i .
paraphrased_text: The CLIP system consists of two key components: a text encoder (referred to as CLIP-T) and an image encoder (referred to as CLIP-ViT). These components are designed to encode input pairs into more closely aligned output embeddings through the utilization of contrastive loss. The resulting trained representation exhibits particular characteristics, as defined by the equation cos(H_i, V_i) for i ≠ j, where H_i represents the output of the CLIP text encoder for input X_i, and V_i represents the output of the CLIP image encoder for input Y_i. The inputs (X_i, Y_i) are considered paired, while any other pair (X_j, Y_k) where j ≠ k is deemed non-paired. Given that both H_i and V_i are normalized to a length of 1, H_i can effectively serve as an approximation of V_i.

title: Mask-then-Fill: A Flexible and Effective Data Augmentation Framework for Event Extraction
human_text: Then, we model DA for the EE task as a Mask-then-Fill process: we first randomly masks out an adjunct sentence fragment and then infills a variable-length text span with a fine-tuned infilling model (T5) (Raffel et al., 2020) . The main advantage lies in that it can replace a fragment of arbitrary length in the text with another fragment of variable length, compared to the existing methods which can only replace a single word or a fixedlength fragment. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to augment training data for event extraction via text infilling.
paraphrased_text: The process for modeling Data Augmentation (DA) in the Event Extraction (EE) task is conceptualized as a Mask-then-Fill procedure. Initially, a random sentence fragment is masked out, followed by the insertion of a text span of varying length using a finely-tuned infilling model (T5) (Raffel et al., 2020). This approach offers a distinct advantage in that it allows for the replacement of text fragments of diverse lengths within the context, in contrast to conventional methods that are limited to substituting single words or fixed-length fragments. Notably, our study represents a novel contribution by introducing text infilling as a means to augment training data for event extraction.

title: Improved Open Source Automatic Subtitling for Lecture Videos
human_text: In semi-automatic generation systems, texts are respoken in a controlled environment by a trained speaker (Sperber et al., 2013; Romero-Fresco, 2020; Vashistha et al., 2017) . However, automatic systems are already being used to subtitle videos and conferences (Milde et al., 2021; Geislinger et al., 2021) . There are several models for German speech recognition available. A model based on Kaldi TDNN-HMM with ARPA rescoring and RNNLM achieved a word error rate (WER) of 7.4% on Tuda-De (Milde, 2022) . The currently lowest WER on Tuda-De is a Conformer Transducer model with 5.8%, which is trained on about 4,600 hours of training data (Wirth and Peinl, 2022) .
