title: A Pipeline for Generating, Annotating and Employing Synthetic Data for Real World Question Answering
human_text: We release this annotation tool and its guidelines for general use. While we use and evaluate this pipeline in the domain of business news, the pipeline is sufficiently flexible to be applied to other domains, including potentially being applicable to abstractive QA. Grammaticality Models allow for improving the quality of synthetic data and subsequent performance in downstream tasks by better aligning it with real user data. On benchmark datasets, such as the Corpus of Linguistic Acceptability (CoLA, Warstadt et al., 2019) which contains a wide range of examples from published linguistics literature, current state-of-the-art models (Sun et al., 2019) can achieve a Matthew's Correlation Coefficient score (Matthews, 1975) of approximately 0.775 (Wang et al., 2022) , exceeding human performance (0.713, Warstadt et al., 2019) in some cases, though this can vary significantly depending on the sentence's syntactic complexity and length (Warstadt and Bowman, 2020) .
paraphrased_text: We introduce an annotation tool and its accompanying guidelines for broad utilization. Although initially developed and tested within the realm of business news, this tool exhibits a high degree of adaptability that renders it suitable for application across various domains, potentially extending to abstractive Question Answering (QA). Grammaticality Models play a crucial role in enhancing the quality of synthetic data, thereby improving the performance of downstream tasks through better alignment with authentic user data. State-of-the-art models, as evidenced by benchmark datasets like the Corpus of Linguistic Acceptability (CoLA, Warstadt et al., 2019), demonstrate impressive performance metrics, with current models achieving a Matthew's Correlation Coefficient score of approximately 0.775 (Wang et al., 2022) on such datasets, surpassing human levels in select instances (0.713, Warstadt et al., 2019). It is worth noting that performance outcomes can be notably influenced by factors such as sentence complexity and length (Warstadt and Bowman, 2020).

title: Joint Alignment of Multi-Task Feature and Label Spaces for Emotion Cause Pair Extraction
human_text: To this end, we in this work propose a feature-task alignment (FTA) scheme of MTL for ECPE (cf. Figure 1(d) ), in which we explicitly split three parts of features, and align them to EE, CE and ECPE respectively. Meanwhile, aligning the label space in a MTL framework is crucial to overall ECPE performance, because intuitively all modules in the MTL process should reach a consensus. For example as in Figure 1 (a), once "c1" is recognized as a cause clause by ECPE module, it should not be further predicted as a non-cause clause by CE module. We notice that such label consistency is not guaranteed in existing MTL-based ECPE methods, which could inevitably hurt the prediction. Therefore we further introduce a inter-task alignment (ITA) mechanism (cf. 1(e)) that learns to pull closer the label distance between the ECPE and the combinations of EE&CE, ensuring label consistency.
paraphrased_text: In this study, we propose a Feature-Task Alignment (FTA) scheme for Multi-Task Learning (MTL) in Event-Centric Pattern Extraction (ECPE), as illustrated in Figure 1(d). The FTA scheme involves the explicit partitioning of features into three distinct parts, each aligned with Event Extraction (EE), Cause Extraction (CE), and ECPE tasks, respectively. Ensuring alignment of the label space within the MTL framework is critical for optimizing ECPE performance, as it is important for all modules in the MTL process to reach a consensus. For instance, as depicted in Figure 1(a), if "c1" is identified as a cause clause by the ECPE module, it should not subsequently be predicted as a non-cause clause by the CE module. It is observed that maintaining label consistency is not a guaranteed aspect in existing MTL-based ECPE methodologies, which could potentially compromise prediction accuracy. Consequently, we introduce an Inter-Task Alignment (ITA) mechanism, as shown in Figure 1(e), which aims to minimize the label discrepancy between ECPE and the combined tasks of EE and CE, thereby ensuring label consistency.

title: CogBERT: Cognition-Guided Pre-trained Language Models
human_text: The lower strands (including phonology, morphology, etc.) work together as the reader becomes accurate and automatic with repetition and practice. Meanwhile, the upper strands (including language structures, semantics, etc.) reinforce each other and weave together with the lower strands to produce a skilled reader. Inspired by previous work, we construct an initial cognitive feature set including 46 fine-grained cognitive features extracted from texts using the spaCy tool (Honnibal and Montani, 2017) and divide them into lower strands features (word-level) and upper strands features (semantic/syntax-level). However, not all cognitive features are statistically significant in eye-tracking data (Zuco 1.0 (Hollenstein et al., 2018) , Zuco 2.0 (Hollenstein et al., 2020) , and Geco (Cop et al., 2016 )). Hence, we filter out cognitive features that the human attention of words with these features is not significantly higher/lower than the average attention of words, retaining 14 usable cognitive features and group them as 8 independent features shown in Table 1 .
paraphrased_text: The different components of reading, such as phonology and morphology, work together as readers become more accurate and automatic through repetition and practice. Meanwhile, higher-level aspects like language structures and semantics support each other and combine with the lower-level components to create a proficient reader. Drawing on previous research, we created an initial set of 46 detailed cognitive features extracted from texts using the spaCy tool (Honnibal and Montani, 2017). These features were categorized into lower-level features (related to individual words) and upper-level features (related to meaning and syntax). However, not all of these cognitive features were found to be statistically significant in eye-tracking data from Zuco 1.0 (Hollenstein et al., 2018), Zuco 2.0 (Hollenstein et al., 2020), and Geco (Cop et al., 2016). Consequently, we filtered out cognitive features that did not result in significantly higher or lower human attention compared to the average attention given to words. This process left us with 14 useful cognitive features, which we grouped into 8 distinct features as shown in Table 1.

title: {R}otate{QVS}: Representing Temporal Information as Rotations in Quaternion Vector Space for Temporal Knowledge Graph Completion
human_text: Previous static KGC works (Sun et al., 2020; Schlichtkrull et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2020) learn explainable embeddings of various relation patterns, so that symmetric pattern (e.g. "co-author"), asymmetric pattern (e.g. "affiliation"), inverse pattern (e.g. "buyer" vs. "seller") and complex composition pattern (e.g. "father's wife (mother)" vs. "wife's father (father in law)") can be captured in static KGs. However, in TKGs, there are inherent connections between entities and their relations along with time-evolving. For example, the relation between Kit Harington and Rose Leslie is in love in 2012, becomes engaged in 2017, and then turns into married in 2018. To the best of our knowledge, very few of the existing TKGE methods can capture them. To address this problem, we take inspirations from Hamilton's quaternion number system (Hamilton, 1844; Zhang et al., 2019a; Gao et al., 2020) and propose a novel method based on quaternion.
paraphrased_text: Previous studies on static Knowledge Graph Completion (KGC) (Sun et al., 2020; Schlichtkrull et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2020) have focused on learning explainable embeddings representing various relation patterns in knowledge graphs. These patterns include symmetric relations (e.g., "co-author"), asymmetric relations (e.g., "affiliation"), inverse relations (e.g., "buyer" vs. "seller"), and complex compositional relations (e.g., "father's wife (mother)" vs. "wife's father (father-in-law)"). However, Temporal Knowledge Graphs (TKGs) introduce a new challenge as the connections between entities and relations evolve over time. For instance, the relationship between Kit Harington and Rose Leslie progresses from being "in love" in 2012, to "engaged" in 2017, and finally to "married" in 2018. Existing Temporal Knowledge Graph Embedding (TKGE) methods are limited in capturing such temporal dynamics. To address this gap, we draw inspiration from Hamilton's quaternion number system (Hamilton, 1844; Zhang et al., 2019a; Gao et al., 2020) and introduce a novel method based on quaternions.

title: Learning to Express in Knowledge-Grounded Conversation
human_text: In general, the expression style of response is composed of two aspects: the structure of the response and the style of the content in each part. As the example shown in Table 1 , knowledge-related phrases and clauses tend to be long, like "And I'd give credit to three different voice actors for anna. ", or short, like "74 in Metacritics". Besides, they may appear at the beginning of the sentence, or at the end. For the sake of description, we decompose a response into a sequence of non-overlapping segments, each is either related to certain background knowledge and diverse in content style, or almost irrelevant to the knowledge but simply playing the role of stitching the context and carrying on the conversation.
paraphrased_text: The style of a response is generally composed of two key aspects: the structure and the content. As illustrated in Table 1, responses often contain knowledge-related phrases and clauses that vary in length, such as the lengthy example "And I'd give credit to three different voice actors for Anna" and the concise "74 in Metacritics." These elements can be found at the beginning or end of a sentence. To better understand the composition of a response, we break it down into distinct segments that either relate to background knowledge and exhibit diverse content styles, or serve to transition the conversation without directly relating to the knowledge being discussed.

title: {F}a{VIQ}: {FA}ct Verification from Information-seeking Questions
human_text: For example, in Figure 1 , the question is ambiguous because the filming of the movie and the release of the movie can both be seen as the creation time. We introduce a new dataset FAVIQ-FAct Verification derived from Information-seeking Questions, which uses such ambiguities to generate challenging fact verification problems. For instance, the claim in Figure 1 requires the model to identify that the movie released in 2001 is in fact filmed in 2000 and to return refute. Like this, claims generated through the crossover of the disambiguation of information-seeking questions are likely to contain misinformation that real users are easily confused with. We automatically generate such claims by composing valid and invalid question-answer pairs and transforming them into textual claims using a neural model.

To address this ambiguity, we present a new dataset called FAVIQ-FAct Verification, which leverages such uncertainties to create challenging fact-checking tasks. We use a neural model to automatically generate these claims by converting valid and invalid question-answer pairs into textual statements.

title: Probing for Constituency Structure in Neural Language Models
human_text: On the one hand, we want to better understand how pre-trained LMs solve certain NLP tasks, i.e., how their input features and neuron activations contribute to a specific classification success. A second motivation is an interest in distributional evidence for linguistic theory. That is, we are interested in assessing which types of linguistic categories emerge when training a contextual language model, i.e., when training a model only on unlabeled text. The research in this paper is primarily motivated by this second aspect, focusing on syntactic structure, more concretely on constituency structure.
paraphrased_text: On one hand, we aim to gain a better understanding of how pre-trained language models (LMs) tackle specific NLP tasks. This involves exploring how their input features and neuron activations contribute to successful classification. Another motivation is to uncover distributional evidence to support linguistic theory. Specifically, we are interested in identifying the linguistic categories that surface during the training of a contextual language model solely on unlabeled text. The research presented in this paper is primarily driven by this second objective, with a focus on syntactic structure, particularly constituency structure.

title: RaP: Redundancy-aware Video-language Pre-training for Text-Video Retrieval
human_text: So we use the minimum dis-similarity between a patch and all tokens as its visual redundancy. Symmetrically, the redundancy of a token depends on how well it corresponds to the patches in the video, and we use the minimum dis-similarity between a token and all patches as its textual redundancy. To reduce the impact of high-redundant patches on learning text embedding or tokens on learning video embedding, we then propose redundancyaware contrastive learning. We take patch-text pairs as additional positives in video-to-text constrastive learning and assign smaller weights to pairs with high-redundant patches. Similarly, we take tokenvideo pairs as additional positives in text-to-video constrastive learning and assign smaller weights to those with high-redundant tokens.
paraphrased_text: The concept of visual and textual redundancy plays a crucial role in our approach. Visual redundancy is determined by calculating the minimum dissimilarity between a patch and all tokens, signifying the extent to which a patch is visually similar to the tokens. Conversely, textual redundancy is evaluated by measuring the minimum dissimilarity between a token and all patches, indicating the degree to which a token corresponds to the visual content in the video. To mitigate the influence of highly redundant patches on text embedding learning and redundant tokens on video embedding learning, we introduce a novel method called redundancy-aware contrastive learning. This approach involves incorporating patch-text pairs as additional positive samples in video-to-text contrastive learning, with reduced weights assigned to pairs containing high-redundancy patches. Likewise, in text-to-video contrastive learning, we include token-video pairs as additional positives, while assigning lower weights to pairs featuring highly redundant tokens.

title: Text Editing as Imitation Game
human_text: As a major obstacle, the training is on state-action demonstrations, where the encoding of the states and actions can be very different (Gu et al., 2018) . For instance, the mismatch of the lengths dimension between the state and action makes it tricky to implement for an auto-regressive modeling that benefits from a single, uniform representation. To tackle the issues above, we reformulate text editing as an imitation game controlled by a Markov Decision Process (MDP). To begin with, we define the input sequence as the initial state, the required operations as action sequences, and the output target sequence as the goal state. A learning agent needs to imitate an expert policy, respond to seen states with actions, and interact with the environment until the success of the eventual editing.
paraphrased_text: A significant challenge in training for natural language processing (NLP) tasks lies in handling state-action demonstrations, which may involve encoding states and actions that differ greatly (Gu et al., 2018). One particular issue is the disparity in dimensionality between the state and action representations, complicating the implementation of auto-regressive models that rely on a consistent, unified representation. To address these challenges, we propose a new approach that reframes text editing as an imitation game governed by a Markov Decision Process (MDP). In this framework, the input sequence serves as the initial state, the required operations are represented as action sequences, and the target output sequence corresponds to the goal state. An intelligent learning agent is then trained to mimic an expert policy, selecting actions in response to observed states and interacting with the environment until the desired editing outcome is achieved.

title: Language Resources to Support Language Diversity -the ELRA Achievements
human_text: A boost in the offer for written LRs can be explained by the ever-growing interest of multilingual corpora and lexica for the improvement of MT Systems which proved a highly re-emerging field over the past few years. From time to time, a number of LRs have been removed from the catalogue for various reasons including contract ending with the provider, new packaging or bad quality, which explains some fluctuations over the years. The proposed approach is partially related to some prior studies of hardness measurement in training deep learning models (Lin et al., 2017; Kalantidis et al., 2020; Hao et al., 2021) , whose focus is to guide the model to pay special attention to hard examples and prevent the vast number of easy negatives from overwhelming the training process. Our approach is inspired by previous studies that focused on measuring the difficulty of training deep learning models (Lin et al., 2017; Kalantidis et al., 2020; Hao et al., 2021). Unlike methods that manually identify difficult examples, our approach does not require distinguishing between easy and hard instances. Nevertheless, since students are often not experts in providing feedback, the instructors need to ensure that the feedback is helpful (Strijbos et al., 2010) . title: Improved Open Source Automatic Subtitling for Lecture Videos
human_text: In semi-automatic generation systems, texts are respoken in a controlled environment by a trained speaker (Sperber et al., 2013; Romero-Fresco, 2020; Vashistha et al., 2017) . However, automatic systems are already being used to subtitle videos and conferences (Milde et al., 2021; Geislinger et al., 2021) . There are several models for German speech recognition available. A model based on Kaldi TDNN-HMM with ARPA rescoring and RNNLM achieved a word error rate (WER) of 7.4% on Tuda-De (Milde, 2022) . The currently lowest WER on Tuda-De is a Conformer Transducer model with 5.8%, which is trained on about 4,600 hours of training data (Wirth and Peinl, 2022) .
paraphrased_text: In semi-automatic speech generation systems, trained speakers re-record texts in a controlled setting. Automatic systems are already being used for subtitling videos and conferences. There are various models available for German speech recognition. For example, a Kaldi TDNN-HMM model with ARPA rescoring and RNNLM achieved a word error rate (WER) of 7.4% on Tuda-De. The most accurate model for Tuda-De currently is a Conformer Transducer model with a WER of 5.8%, trained on approximately 4,600 hours of data.

title: Guiding Neural Entity Alignment with Compatibility
human_text: Neural EA (Sun et al., 2020b; Zhao et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2020) is current mainstream direction which emerges with the development of deep learning techniques. Various neural architectures have been introduced to encode entities. Translation-based KG encoders were explored at the start (Chen et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2017) . Though these models could capture the structure information, they were not capable of incorporating attribute information. Graph Convolutional Network (GCN)-based encoders later became the mainstream method because they were flexible in combining different types of information and achieved higher performance (Wang et al., 2018; Cao et al., 2019; Mao et al., 2020a; Sun et al., 2020a; Mao et al., 2020b) .
paraphrased_text: The current mainstream direction in knowledge graph encoding is Neural EA, which has emerged with the advancement of deep learning techniques. Initially, translation-based KG encoders, as explored by Chen et al. (2017) and Zhu et al. (2017), could capture structural information but lacked the ability to incorporate attribute information. Subsequently, Graph Convolutional Network (GCN)-based encoders gained popularity as they were more flexible in integrating various types of information and achieved superior performance, as demonstrated by Wang et al. (2018), Cao et al. (2019), Mao et al. (2020a), Sun et al. (2020a), and Mao et al. (2020b).

title: Automatic Translation Alignment for Ancient Greek and Latin
human_text: The DFHG is the digital open version of the five volumes of the first big printed collection of ancient Greek fragmentary historians edited by Karl Müller in the 19th century . The collection gathers more than eight thousand quotations and text-reuses (fragments) of lost works written by more than six hundred authors ranging from the 6th century BC through the 7th century CE (Berti, 2019a; Berti, 2021) . Fragments are extracted from still extant source texts and are generally constituted by short passages with information about the relevant lost author and work. Almost every Greek fragment is translated or shortened into Latin. Limits are of course represented by the fact that the Latin of the corpus is the language used by philologists in the 19th century and not the language of ancient sources.
paraphrased_text: The DFHG, or Digital Fragmenta Historicorum Graecorum, is a digital open version of the five volumes of the first major printed collection of ancient Greek fragmentary historians edited by Karl Müller in the 19th century. This collection compiles over eight thousand quotations and text reuses (fragments) from lost works by more than six hundred authors spanning from the 6th century BC to the 7th century CE (Berti, 2019a; Berti, 2021). These fragments are extracted from existing source texts and typically consist of brief passages containing information about the respective lost author and work. Nearly every Greek fragment is either translated or summarized in Latin. It is important to note that the Latin language used in the corpus reflects the linguistic practices of 19th-century philologists and not the language of the original ancient sources.

title: Benchmarking Answer Verification Methods for Question Answering-Based Summarization Evaluation Metrics
human_text: First, a set of questions is automatically generated from one text. Then, those questions are automatically answered against a second text to obtain a set of predicted answers. The final score is proportional to the number of correct predictions, but determining whether those predictions are correct (the task of answer verification) is done by comparing the text of the prediction to the expected answer. Therefore, instead of directly comparing the entire contents of the two texts, QA-based metrics instead reduce the scope of the problem to only comparing specific pairs of phrases. Current QA-based metrics perform the answer verification step by lexical comparison, either exact match or token F 1 .
paraphrased_text: Initially, a series of inquiries is algorithmically formulated based on one text. Subsequently, these inquiries are systematically addressed in reference to a different text to derive a collection of projected responses. The ultimate evaluation metric is commensurate with the accuracy of these responses; however, the process of determining their accuracy (referred to as answer verification) involves juxtaposing the prediction text with the anticipated response. Thus, QA-based evaluation metrics streamline the assessment by narrowing the focus to specific phrase pairs rather than conducting a comprehensive comparison of the entire texts. Presently, QA-based metrics execute the answer verification phase through lexical scrutiny, employing either exact matching or token F1 scoring.

title: From Rewriting to Remembering: Common Ground for Conversational {QA} Models
human_text: At each turn, the relevant information is distilled in one or more propositions, which are added to the CG. As a new question comes in, the model selects the relevant information in the CG, and uses it to answer the question. The CG can thus be considered as an optimized summary, which returns the relevant information at every turn while keeping all the information discussed so far. We use the QReCC dataset (Anantha et al., 2020 ) to test CG on the task of Open-Domain Conversational QA (ODCQA) -in which answers to questions in a conversation have to be found in a large collection of documents -and show that it improves over existing approaches for modelling conversational information. We show that this is due to the fact that CG implements a more efficient and human-like way to account for previous information, which takes the best of existing approaches while avoiding their shortcomings: on the one hand, CG can access and maintain the full previous conversational context, but it avoids the noise issue; on the other, it can distill relevant information, but it is not forced to compress it in a single rewrite.
paraphrased_text: At each juncture, pertinent information is succinctly encapsulated in one or more propositions that are integrated into the conversational graph (CG). Upon the introduction of a new query, the model identifies the pertinent data within the CG and utilizes it to address the query. The CG can be viewed as an optimized synopsis that furnishes the relevant data at each juncture while retaining all previously discussed information. Leveraging the QReCC dataset (Anantha et al., 2020), we evaluate the efficacy of CG in the realm of Open-Domain Conversational Question Answering (ODCQA), where responses to queries within a dialogue must be sourced from an expansive document repository. Our findings demonstrate that CG outperforms existing methodologies in modeling conversational data. This superiority is attributed to CG's implementation of a more efficient and human-like methodology for incorporating preceding information, amalgamating the strengths of existing approaches while circumventing their drawbacks. Specifically, CG can access and uphold the entire preceding conversational context without succumbing to noise-related issues, while also distilling pertinent information without being constrained to compress it into a singular revision.

title: CHECKHARD: Checking Hard Labels for Adversarial Text Detection, Prediction Correction, and Perturbed Word Suggestion
human_text: Zhou et al. ( 2019 ) fine-tuned a BERT model to suggest perturbed words. Mozes et al. (2021) suggested lowfrequency words as perturbed words. However, many benign words are also suggested in addition to the perturbed words. This redundant suggestion affects the adversarial text detection and prediction correction in downstream tasks.
paraphrased_text: In a study by Zhou et al. (2019), a BERT model was fine-tuned to recommend altered words. Additionally, Mozes et al. (2021) proposed using low-frequency words as altered words. However, these altered word suggestions include many harmless words alongside the intended changes. This overlap can impact the accuracy of detecting adversarial text and making corrections in subsequent tasks.

title: A Speculative and Tentative Common Ground Handling for Efficient Composition of Uncertain Dialogue
human_text: Even if the specific object they refer to is not explicitly indicated, the work can be carried out smoothly without additional inquiry because they have established the belief that the object they refer to is the same thing/issue (Clark & Carlson, 1982) . This belief arises from common ground between the participants that has been acquired through years of trial and error. Clark and Schaefer consider dialogue a cooperative work process by participants toward a goal, and they explained the degree of establishing common ground through their "contribution model" (Clark & Schaefer, 1989 ). The contribution model has two stages, presentation and acceptance, and it is thought that the grounding is achieved through the joint action of the participants through these stages. However, although the contribution model shows a qualitative procedure of belief grounding among participants, it does not sufficiently explain its computational grounding procedure.
paraphrased_text: Even without explicit mention of a specific object, smooth progress can be made in the work, as individuals often share a mutual understanding that the object in question is the same (Clark & Carlson, 1982). This shared belief stems from a common foundation established through years of collaboration and experience. Clark and Schaefer view dialogue as a collaborative effort among participants working towards a common goal, as outlined in their "contribution model" that illustrates the process of establishing common ground (Clark & Schaefer, 1989). The contribution model consists of two key stages - presentation and acceptance - where participants collectively work to solidify their shared understanding. While the contribution model provides insight into the qualitative aspects of belief grounding, it does not fully address the computational procedures involved in this process.

title: Characterizing Verbatim Short-Term Memory in Neural Language Models
human_text: What is the nature of the memory processes that LMs learn? Are these memory processes able to access individual tokens from the recent past verbatim, or is the memory system more implicit, so that only an aggregate gist of the prior context is available to subsequent processing? Here, we introduce a paradigm (Fig. 1 ), inspired by benchmark tasks for models of human shortterm memory (Oberauer et al., 2018) , for characterizing short-term memory abilities of LMs. We apply it to two particular neural LM architectures that possess the architectural ingredients to hold past items in memory: attention-based transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) and long short-term memory networks (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997, LSTM) . Whereas LSTMs incorporate the past by reusing the results of processing from previous time steps through dedicated memory cells, transformers use the internal representations of each of the previous tokens as input.
paraphrased_text: What are the underlying memory mechanisms that language models (LMs) acquire? Do these mechanisms enable direct access to specific tokens from recent history, or do they rely on implicit memory processes that capture a general understanding of prior context for subsequent processing? To explore the short-term memory capabilities of LMs, we introduce a framework (see Fig. 1) inspired by established tasks used to study human short-term memory models (Oberauer et al., 2018). This framework is applied to two distinct neural LM architectures known for their ability to retain past information: attention-based transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) and long short-term memory networks (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997, LSTM). While LSTMs preserve past information by leveraging memory cells to store and reuse processed data from earlier time steps, transformers utilize the internal representations of each preceding token as input.

title: Joint Completion and Alignment of Multilingual Knowledge Graphs
human_text: Massive KGs like WikiData cover well-resourced and resource-poor languages. Cross-lingual transfer thus acquires increased utility in the context of Linked Open Data initiatives. Different KGs will assign a different ID, and often also a different surface form, to the same entity, such as "Estados Unidos de América" for the USA entity in a Spanish KG. This leads to ID proliferation. Recent work on entity alignment (EA) across KGs attempts to assign a unique ID to all nodes representing the same entity (Chen et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2017 Sun et al., , 2018 Sun et al., , 2020c;; Cao et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2021; Tang et al., 2020) .
paraphrased_text: Large knowledge graphs like WikiData cover languages with varying levels of resources. In this context, cross-lingual transfer becomes increasingly valuable in Linked Open Data initiatives. Different knowledge graphs will assign unique IDs and often different names to the same entity, like using "Estados Unidos de América" for the USA entity in a Spanish knowledge graph. This results in a proliferation of IDs. Recent research on entity alignment across knowledge graphs aims to assign a single ID to all nodes that represent the same entity (Chen et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2017, 2018, 2020; Cao et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2021; Tang et al., 2020).

title: MAGMA -Multimodal Augmentation of Generative Models through Adapter-based Finetuning
human_text: Our work builds on recent advances in parameter efficient finetuning of LMs (Houlsby et al., 2019; Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021; He et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2021) , specifically with adapter layers (Houlsby et al., 2019; He et al., 2021; Pfeiffer et al., 2020) , which are small modules inserted in between the elements of a transformer layer which are finetuned instead of the model weights as a form of parameter efficient fine-tuning. For a visual backbone, it is common to use region features from a pretrained object detection model such as Faster- RCNN (Ren et al., 2015) . These are generally trained using expensive human labeled data on a bounded set of object classes, limiting the number of object types the resulting model can recognize.
paraphrased_text: Our work is based on recent advancements in efficiently fine-tuning language models (LMs) using adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019; Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021; He et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2021). Adapters are small modules inserted between transformer layers for fine-tuning instead of adjusting the model weights directly, which helps in parameter-efficient fine-tuning. To provide a visual backbone, it is common to incorporate region features from a pretrained object detection model like Faster-RCNN (Ren et al., 2015). These models are typically trained on costly human-labeled data for a limited number of object classes, which restricts the variety of objects they can accurately identify.

title: A New Dataset for Topic-Based Paragraph Classification in Genocide-Related Court Transcripts
human_text: Beyond the contribution to the NLP community, it is our hope that the results of this paper will be useful for both scholars and practitioners at international criminal tribunals who need to work through large quantities of transcript material as part of their everyday job. We summarize our contributions as follows: 1. We present GTC, a new reference corpus sampled from different international criminal courts in the context of genocide tribunals. The corpus contains annotations of statements by witnesses about experienced violence. 2. We built state-of-the-art transformer-based classifiers to provide benchmarks for the new classification task of paragraph identification of violencerelated witness statements.
paraphrased_text: It is our hope that the results of this paper will not only benefit the NLP community but also provide valuable assistance to scholars and practitioners at international criminal tribunals who regularly deal with large amounts of transcript material. Our contributions can be summarized as follows: 1. Introducing GTC, a novel reference corpus sourced from various international criminal courts focusing on genocide tribunals. This corpus includes annotations of witness statements regarding encountered violence. 2. We have developed cutting-edge transformer-based classifiers to establish benchmarks for the novel classification task of identifying paragraphs related to violence within witness statements.

title: GiCCS: A German in-Context Conversational Similarity Benchmark
human_text: STS has been used both for training (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019; Vulic et al., 2021) and for evaluating LMs (Yang et al., 2018) . To the best of our knowledge, the majority of STS benchmarks have been created from written language resources using non-conversational data, and are mainly in English. Conversational data, however, has some peculiarities which make it potentially challenging for the STS task: (1) questions and requests are frequent, (2) whether two sentences are semantically similar may depend on pragmatic factors triggered by the conversational context. For example, it may be challenging for human annotators to assess that Could you turn it up a bit? and I'd like the AC to be colder advance the conversation in a similar way, without (1) sufficient conversational context and (2) an operative definition of what it means for a question and a declarative sentence to be semantically similar.
paraphrased_text: The majority of STS benchmarks have been created from written language resources in English and have been used for training and evaluating language models. However, conversational data presents unique challenges for the STS task. Conversational data often includes questions and requests, and the semantic similarity of two sentences may depend on pragmatic factors influenced by the conversational context. For instance, determining whether "Could you turn it up a bit?" and "I'd like the AC to be colder" advance the conversation similarly can be difficult without adequate conversational context and a clear definition of semantic similarity between a question and a declarative sentence.

title: A Double-Graph Based Framework for Frame Semantic Parsing
human_text: We propose two ways of reasoning about frame semantic parsing: inter-frame reasoning and intraframe reasoning in Figure 2 . Frame knowledge mentioned above can guide both ways of reasoning. The frame semantic relation between Receiving and Getting and FE mappings associated with it allow us to learn from the left sentence when parsing the right sentence because similar argument spans of two sentences will have related FEs as their roles. The FE definitions reflect dependencies between arguments. The definition of Role in frame Receiving mentions Theme and Donor, which reflects dependencies between argument the book and argument as a gift.
paraphrased_text: In Figure 2, we present two approaches to frame semantic parsing: inter-frame reasoning and intraframe reasoning. The frame knowledge discussed earlier serves as a guide for both of these reasoning methods. For example, the frame semantic relation between "Receiving" and "Getting," along with their associated Frame Element (FE) mappings, enables us to leverage information from the left sentence to parse the right sentence. This is because similar argument spans in both sentences are likely to have related FEs assigned to them. The definitions of FEs reveal the dependencies between arguments. For instance, in the definition of the role "Role" in the "Receiving" frame, the mention of "Theme" and "Donor" illustrates the dependencies between the argument "the book" and the argument "as a gift."

title: Complex Reading Comprehension Through Question Decomposition
human_text: Despite significant research on RC (Dua et al., 2019; Perez et al., 2020) , those questions that require strong compositional generalisability and numerical reasoning abilities are still challenging to even the state-of-the-art models (Ran et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020a,b; Wei et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2022) . While decomposition is a natural approach to tackle this problem, the lack of sufficient groundtruth sub-questions limits our ability to train RC models based on large LMs. In this paper, we propose a novel low-budget (only 1‰ parameters of GPT-3) learning approach to improve LMs' performance on hard multi-hop RC such as the Break subset of DROP (Dua et al., 2019) . Our model consists of two main modules: (1) an encoder-decoder LM as a question decomposer and (2) another encoder-decoder LM as the reading comprehension model. First, we train the question decomposer to decompose a difficult multi-hop question to sub-questions from a limited amount of annotated data.
paraphrased_text: Despite extensive research on reading comprehension (RC) as evidenced by studies conducted by Dua et al. (2019) and Perez et al. (2020), questions that necessitate strong compositional generalizability and numerical reasoning skills continue to pose challenges even for state-of-the-art models, as indicated by the works of Ran et al. (2019), Chen et al. (2020a, 2020b), Wei et al. (2022), and Zhou et al. (2022). While decomposition presents a viable strategy for addressing this issue, the scarcity of groundtruth sub-questions hampers our capacity to effectively train RC models using large language models (LMs). This study introduces a novel low-budget learning methodology, utilizing only 1% of the parameters of GPT-3, to enhance the performance of LMs on complex multi-hop RC tasks such as the Break subset of the DROP dataset (Dua et al., 2019). Our proposed model comprises two primary components: (1) an encoder-decoder LM serving as a question decomposer and (2) another encoder-decoder LM functioning as the reading comprehension model. Initially, the question decomposer is trained to break down intricate multi-hop questions into sub-questions using a limited volume of annotated data.

title: {N}ibbling at the Hard Core of {W}ord {S}ense {D}isambiguation
human_text: Similarly, in the context below, another system which breaks the 80% performance ceiling (Conia and Navigli, 2021 ) makes a trivial mistake on a standard test instance (Snyder and Palmer, 2004) , and fails to label the word couple properly: context: I was just sitting down to meet with some new therapy clients, a couple, and the building started shaking (. . . ) gold: A pair of people who live together. Conia and Navigli (2021): A small indefinite number. With a view to gaining a better understanding of the nature of what systems still fail to disambiguate, in this work we provide the following main contributions: (i) we put forward a detailed quantitative and qualitative analysis of errors shared among seven state-of-the-art systems for English WSD, including systems that have surpassed the 80% human estimate in terms of F1 score (Bevilacqua and Navigli, 2020; Barba et al., 2021a) , (ii) we produce an amended version of the English all-words WSD evaluation benchmarks featured in Senseval and SemEval tasks (Agirre et al., 2009; Raganato et al., 2017a) , (iii) we devise "42D" (pron. [for•ti•tude]), the first manually-curated test bed made available to the research community after a hiatus of seven years since SemEval-2015 (Moro and Navigli, 2015) , and a powerful evaluation tool for estimating system resilience in contexts featuring least frequent word senses, (iv) we establish a new human performance threshold for assessing actual superhuman scores on WSD test sets, and propose macro-averaged F1 score as an alternative to micro-averaged F1 score to better account for least frequent word senses in WSD evaluation, (v) we release "hardEN", a challenge set for English all-words WSD on which state-of-the-art systems under investigation achieve exactly 0.0% F1 score, and (vi) we set up an experimental setting to show the impact sense distribution has over the aforementioned datasets.
paraphrased_text:
In the given context, one system that surpasses the 80% performance threshold (Conia and Navigli, 2021) makes a minor error in a standard test case (Snyder and Palmer, 2004) by incorrectly labeling the word "couple." While the gold standard defines "couple" as "A pair of people who live together," the system labels it as "A small indefinite number." To gain deeper insights into the challenges that systems face in word sense disambiguation (WSD), this work presents the following key contributions: 

(i) A comprehensive analysis of errors shared among seven cutting-edge English WSD systems, including those that have exceeded the 80% human benchmark in terms of F1 score (Bevilacqua and Navigli, 2020; Barba et al., 2021a). 

(ii) An updated version of the English all-words WSD evaluation benchmarks used in Senseval and SemEval tasks (Agirre et al., 2009; Raganato et al., 2017a). 

(iii) Introduction of "42D" (pronounced as [for•ti•tude]), a meticulously curated test suite made available to the research community after a seven-year hiatus since SemEval-2015 (Moro and Navigli, 2015), designed to assess system resilience in contexts with the least frequent word senses. 

(iv) Establishment of a new human performance threshold to evaluate actual superhuman scores on WSD test sets, with a proposal to use macro-averaged F1 score instead of micro-averaged F1 score to better address the least frequent word senses in WSD evaluation. 

(v) Introduction of "hardEN," a challenge dataset for English all-words WSD where state-of-the-art systems achieve a 0.0% F1 score.

(vi) Implementation of an experimental framework to illustrate the impact of sense distribution on the aforementioned datasets.

title: Team {LEGO} at {S}em{E}val-2022 Task 4: Machine Learning Methods for {PCL} Detection
human_text: Support Vector Machine (Burges, 1998) is an effective technique for classifying high dimensional data. It learns the optimal hyperplane that separates training examples from different classes by maximizing the classification margin. Each row vector of the word-document matrix represents the vectorization of text that are mapped to a latent semantic space in this module by LSA vector space model, to generate representation vectors and further classify them. We perform Principal Component Analysis (PCA) (number of components=500) to perform dimension reduction over text features before inputting to this model. (Cramer, 2002) is a classification algorithm used to solve binary and multilabel classification.
paraphrased_text:
The Support Vector Machine (SVM) model, proposed by Burges in 1998, is an effective technique for classifying high-dimensional data. It works by learning the optimal hyperplane that separates training examples from different classes, with the goal of maximizing the classification margin. In this approach, each row vector in the word-document matrix represents the vectorized text, which is then mapped to a latent semantic space using the Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA) vector space model. This process generates representation vectors that are further classified.

Before inputting the text features into the SVM model, we apply Principal Component Analysis (PCA) with 500 components to reduce the dimensionality of the data. Additionally, the Cramer algorithm (2002) is utilized for binary and multilabel classification tasks.

title: Leveraging Mental Health Forums for User-level Depression Detection on Social Media
human_text: We focus on Reddit as it is a unique platform on which users can choose to create "throwaway" accounts that are not associated with their main accounts. This allows them to feel safe to make posts or comments disclosing sensitive information without revealing their identity. We believe this simulates the way people suffering from depression typically share their experiences online. This also grants the added advantage of allowing longer post lengths in contrast to platforms such as Twitter and Instagram, thus being more helpful to our user-level classification task, which hugely relies on context. The Reddit Self-reported Depression Diagnosis (RSDD) dataset (Yates et al., 2017) was selected for this study as it simulated challenges one would face when dealing with the vastness of social media on limited resources, as well as the class imbalance of depressed vs other miscellaneous content.
paraphrased_text:
Our research focuses on the unique characteristics of Reddit as a platform that offers users the option to create anonymous "throwaway" accounts distinct from their primary accounts. This feature enables individuals to share sensitive information without disclosing their true identities, fostering a sense of safety and confidentiality. We posit that this setting mirrors the online disclosure patterns observed among individuals experiencing depression. Furthermore, the extended post length permitted on Reddit, as opposed to platforms like Twitter and Instagram, enhances the utility of the platform for our user-level classification task, which heavily relies on contextual information.

In this study, we leverage the Reddit Self-reported Depression Diagnosis (RSDD) dataset developed by Yates et al. (2017) due to its ability to replicate challenges encountered in analyzing social media data within resource-constrained environments. Additionally, the dataset's representation of the class imbalance between depressed and non-depressed content aligns with the complexities present in real-world social media data analysis.

title: What a Creole Wants, What a Creole Needs
human_text: But the only way we can learn this, claims Bird (2020) , is by establishing a respectful, "feedback/collaboration loop", and necessarily involving community members in our research. Following the work of Bird (2020) , in this work, we focus on the problem of creating resources for lowresource languages, in this case Creoles, and the inherent presupposition by researchers of what technologies are indeed wanted and needed by the communities speaking those languages. While many researchers may assume that the "best-case scenario" for all languages would be to have all language technologies equally available, the fact of the matter is that many communities have very specific wants and needs of language technology, as well as language technologies that are notably unwelcome, even though they are a commonplace for high-resource languages. Disregarding the needs of a language community can lead to misuse of finite resources on creating unnecessary datasets or technologies while leaving the community's highest priorities neglected. And finally, when researchers assume what technologies are wanted on the behalf of a community, it inherently alienates that community and takes away their agency (Bird, 2020) .
paraphrased_text:
According to Bird (2020), the key to learning lies in establishing a respectful "feedback/collaboration loop" that involves community members in our research endeavors. This approach underscores the importance of understanding the specific needs of language communities, particularly in the context of developing resources for low-resource languages like Creoles. Oftentimes, researchers may assume that all languages would benefit from having equal access to language technologies. However, the reality is that different communities have distinct preferences and priorities when it comes to language technologies, and some technologies that are common in high-resource languages may not be welcomed in others.

Neglecting the unique needs of a language community can result in misallocation of resources towards creating unnecessary datasets or technologies, while failing to address the community's most pressing concerns. Moreover, when researchers make assumptions about the desired technologies on behalf of a community, it can lead to the community feeling alienated and disempowered. By actively involving community members in the research process and prioritizing their input, researchers can ensure that their efforts are aligned with the actual needs and preferences of the communities they aim to serve (Bird, 2020).

title: {DLRG}@{LT}-{EDI}-{ACL}2022:Detecting signs of Depression from Social Media using {XGB}oost Method
human_text: SVM iteratively generates the best hyperplane, which is then utilised to minimise an error. The goal of SVM is to find a maximum marginal hyperplane (MMH) that splits a dataset into classes as evenly as possible. A hyperplane is a decision plane that divides a group of items that belong to various classes. A margin is the distance between the two lines on the class points that are closest to each other. Random Forest (RF): Random Forest is a classifier that combines a number of decision trees on different subsets of a dataset and averages the results to increase the dataset's predicted accuracy.
paraphrased_text:
Support Vector Machine (SVM) is an iterative algorithm that aims to determine the optimal hyperplane to minimize classification errors. The fundamental objective of SVM is to identify the Maximum Margin Hyperplane (MMH) that effectively segregates a given dataset into distinct classes. A hyperplane serves as a dividing boundary that allocates data points into specific categories, while the margin represents the spatial gap between the nearest data points of different classes. 

On the other hand, Random Forest (RF) operates as an ensemble classifier that amalgamates multiple decision trees constructed from different data subsets. By aggregating the outputs of these individual trees, Random Forest enhances the predictive accuracy of the model on the overall dataset.

title: Refining an Almost Clean Translation Memory Helps Machine Translation
human_text: Progress in deep learning methods recently led to a number of classifiers trained without feature engineering. Notably, Grégoire and Langlais (2018) describe a siamese recurrent neural network that encodes source and target sentences into vectors that are then fed through a non-linear transformation in order to classify a sentence pair as parallel or not. The authors show that training such a model yields better performance than the aforementioned approach, and that adding parallel material extracted from Wikipedia using this model leads to systematic (although modest) gains in both statistical and neural machine translation performance. While these studies convincingly show that parallel sentences can be mined from comparable corpora, it remains unclear whether these methods are also useful for filtering out noise from a relatively clean translation memory. Filtering Out Noise: In an early attempt to tackle this issue, Macklovitch (1994) used simple heuristics to detect specific problems observed in real (professional) translations, such as errors in numerical entities, calques, or abnormal translation sizes.
paraphrased_text:
Recent advancements in deep learning have enabled the development of classifiers that do not require feature engineering. For instance, Grégoire and Langlais (2018) introduced a siamese recurrent neural network that transforms source and target sentences into vectors. These vectors undergo a non-linear transformation to determine whether a sentence pair is parallel or not. The researchers demonstrated that training this model resulted in improved performance compared to traditional methods. Additionally, incorporating parallel content from Wikipedia using this model led to consistent, albeit modest, improvements in both statistical and neural machine translation performance.

While these studies effectively showcase the ability to extract parallel sentences from comparable corpora, it remains uncertain whether these methods can effectively filter out noise from a relatively clean translation memory. In a previous effort to address this challenge, Macklovitch (1994) utilized simple heuristics to identify specific issues commonly found in real (professional) translations, such as errors in numerical values, calques, or discrepancies in translation lengths.

title: Computational Referencing System for Sanskrit Grammar
human_text: A computer system for Sanskrit grammar based on the text of AD has developed to store and make accessible it in a interactive way with user friendly format. In the present time five kinds of CL research work for Sanskrit based on is in process. These are the following types: 1. analysis and description of Sanskrit structure; 2. developing learning/teaching systems within the Pāṇinian frame; 3. inferring from AD; 4. its study as a primary model of knowledge (semiotics). computerizing and storing the text; The last one area may be modified into the study and storing of AD as a text/as a Śāstra. A full access to the text will be made possible by reconstituting or organizing it into a computer text system.
paraphrased_text:
A computer system has been developed to store and provide interactive access to the text of Ashtadhyayi, a foundational Sanskrit grammar text. This system is designed with a user-friendly interface to facilitate easy navigation and learning. Currently, there are five ongoing research projects focusing on different aspects of Sanskrit grammar based on Ashtadhyayi:

1. Analysis and description of Sanskrit structure
2. Development of learning and teaching systems based on the principles of Panini
3. Drawing inferences from Ashtadhyayi
4. Studying Ashtadhyayi as a primary model of knowledge (semiotics)
5. Computerizing and storing the text for easy access and analysis

The final area of research aims to expand into studying and storing Ashtadhyayi as a text and as a Shastra. By reconstituting and organizing the text into a computerized system, full access to its content will be made possible for scholars and enthusiasts alike.

title: CorEDs: a Corpus on Eating Disorders
human_text: Our goal was to create a corpus on EDs that could be used for various types of analyses, being at the same time easily adaptable for different machine learning tasks. In order to accomplish this, we focused on the American social news website and forum Reddit, a discussion website where registered members submit contents such as links, text posts, images, and videos, which are then voted up or down by other members. Registrations is free and posts are organized by subject into user-created boards called "communities" or "subreddits", which cover a wide variety of topics and can be accessed via keyword search. The reason why we selected this platform is twofold: on the one hand, not imposing any limitation in the length of the posts, Reddit makes available longer and more complex texts (compared for example with the largely investigated Twitter, where the maximum length of a post is 280 characters); on the other hand, the discussion-oriented nature of the website also naturally leads to more articulated and linguistically rich comments. For these reasons, the type of linguistic data that can be extracted from Reddit appears to be suitable for the task at hand, that is building a multi-purpose corpus on EDs.
paraphrased_text:
Our aim was to create a corpus focused on eating disorders (EDs) that could be utilized for various types of analyses and easily adapted for different machine learning tasks. To achieve this, we concentrated on Reddit, an American social news website and forum. Reddit is a platform where registered members share content such as links, text posts, images, and videos, which are then voted up or down by other members. Registration is free, and posts are categorized by subject into user-created boards known as "communities" or "subreddits," covering a wide range of topics and accessible via keyword search.

We chose Reddit for two main reasons: firstly, it does not impose any restrictions on the length of posts, allowing for longer and more complex texts compared to platforms like Twitter, which have a character limit of 280. Secondly, the discussion-focused nature of Reddit naturally encourages more detailed and linguistically rich comments. Therefore, the linguistic data available on Reddit is well-suited for creating a versatile corpus on eating disorders.

title: Large Scale Substitution-based Word Sense Induction
human_text: These contextualized vectors proved to be very effective as features for downstream NLP tasks. However, contextualized embeddings also have some major shortcomings: most notably for our case, they are expensive to store (e.g. BERT embeddings are 768 or 1024 floating point numbers for each token), and are hard to index and query at scale. Even if we do manage to store and query them, they are not interpretable, making it impossible for a user to query for a particular sense of a word without providing a full disambiguating context for that word. For example, consider a user wishing to query a dataset for sentences discussing Oracle in the mythology-prophet sense, rather than the tech company sense. It is not clear how to formulate such a query to an index of contextualized word vectors.
paraphrased_text:
These contextualized vectors have proven to be highly effective as features for downstream NLP tasks. However, they also come with significant drawbacks. Most notably, in our case, they are costly to store (e.g., BERT embeddings consist of 768 or 1024 floating-point numbers for each token) and challenging to index and query at scale. Even if we successfully store and query them, they lack interpretability, preventing users from querying for specific senses of a word without providing a complete disambiguating context for that word. 

For instance, imagine a user wanting to search a dataset for sentences discussing "Oracle" in the mythological-prophet sense rather than the tech company sense. The process of formulating such a query to an index of contextualized word vectors is not straightforward.

The limitations of existing datasets have a direct impact on the accuracy and robustness of the models used in Sign Language Recognition (SLR). Recently developed datasets have been designed to overcome the challenges faced by previous datasets. For example, BSL-1K (Albanie et al., 2020) offers the largest collection of annotated sign data, while How2Sign (Duarte et al., 2021) boasts the largest vocabulary size. These datasets have improved upon older ones, which were often recorded in controlled lab settings or extracted from TV broadcasts.

In this context, the KRSL-OnlineSchool dataset stands out as it includes a large vocabulary size and comprehensive gloss annotation crucial for training recognition models. By addressing these key aspects, this dataset contributes significantly to the advancement of sign language research and its application in real-world scenarios.

However, if the character's name is unknown when he/she first appears, the node's  • The character's name referring to the same character entity will be consistent for both the global character relationship labelling and the referential relationship labelling(see sec 2.2.2). • All the relationships between each pair of character entities should be annotated. paraphrased_text:
The following set of rules outlines the procedures to be followed in creating a coherent graph representation of character relationships in the script:

1. Each character introduced in the script will be added as a new node in the graph. The node will be labeled with the character's name. In cases where a character's name is unknown at first appearance, a placeholder can be used until the name is revealed.

2. Consistency is key in labeling character relationships. The same name should be used to refer to a specific character entity across both the global character relationship labeling and the referential relationship labeling (refer to section 2.2.2).

3. It is essential to annotate all relationships between pairs of character entities in the graph. Additionally, timestamps should be added to indicate when these relationships first emerge, as character dynamics may evolve and change over time.

title: A Quality Estimation and Quality Evaluation Tool for the Translation Industry
human_text: MTQA Tool Overview: The MTQA is designed as a standalone tool and an API that can be used by users or invoked by other tools. Behind the user interface lies a distributed architecture which operates in two modes. Intermediate and final results are displayed to the user; final results are made available for download. MTQEv is a human-driven quality assessment module, in which one or more MT systems' quality is evaluated based on a human-generated translation reference. MTQEv is typically used to compare already-in-use and new MT models by means of comparing their translation to the human gold standard, using automatic metrics, such as TER (Snover et al., 2006) , BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002) , chrF (Popović, 2015) and others.
paraphrased_text:
MTQA Tool Description

The MT Quality Assessment (MTQA) tool functions both as a standalone application and an Application Programming Interface (API) that can be utilized by end users or integrated into other software tools. Operating on a distributed architecture, the tool offers two distinct modes of operation. Users are presented with both intermediate and final assessment results, with the latter being downloadable for further analysis.

The MTQEv component of the tool is a module dedicated to human-driven evaluation of machine translation (MT) systems. This evaluation process involves comparing the quality of one or more MT systems against a reference translation produced by a human. Typically, MTQEv is employed to assess the performance of existing and new MT models by measuring their translations against a human-generated gold standard. This comparison is facilitated by the use of automated metrics such as Translation Error Rate (TER) (Snover et al., 2006), Bilingual Evaluation Understudy (BLEU) (Papineni et al., 2002), chrF (Popović, 2015), among others.

title: A Meta-framework for Spatiotemporal Quantity Extraction from Text
human_text: Second, we need to define the boundaries of these quantity spans. For instance, in "five cases in Seattle," should one label the text span of "five" or "five cases"? What about "4.8 billion" and "$4.8 billion"? Similar to labeling an event using its predicate only, our choice is to keep the span minimal while keeping the numerical semantics: we will mark "five" (i.e., drop "case"), "4.8 billion" (i.e., keep "billion"), and "4.8 billion" (i.e., drop "$") in these examples. Minimising the span does not lose information about the quantity-only marking "five" in "five cases" does not prevent us from identifying its type, unit, and spatiotemporal extent in subsequent annotation tasks.
paraphrased_text: When defining quantity spans, it's important to establish clear boundaries. For example, in the phrase "five cases in Seattle," should we focus on the term "five" or "five cases"? Similarly, when dealing with figures like "4.8 billion," should we include the currency symbol "$" or not? To maintain clarity while capturing numerical meaning, we opt to highlight "five" (omitting "cases"), "4.8 billion" (retaining "billion"), and "$4.8 billion" (excluding "$") in such instances. By minimizing the span, we can still convey essential information without losing details necessary for identifying the type, unit, and context of the quantity.

title: A Domain Knowledge Enhanced Pre-Trained Language Model for Vertical Search: Case Study on Medicinal Products
human_text: Most of the models trained on either the general corpus or the medical literature corpus lack medicinal product knowledge. For the product vertical search scenario, medicinal product information is usually stored in structured relational database tables, and traditional pretrained language models focus on natural language text in the form of sentences without considering the semantic relationship modeling of structured text in the product information tables. From the perspective of users' search habits, in addition to searching directly for the medicine name, users also often search for disease, symptoms and other important proudct attribute words to find the medicine. Therefore, we propose a novel pre-training task called product attribute prediction (PAP). Although we could carefully craft a medicinal product knowledge graph from product databases and then try to inject the extracted explicit knowledge graph into the pre-trained language model, we argue that the procedure of building product knowledge graph is laborious but avoidable by training the language model directly on product structural information.
paraphrased_text:
Many existing models trained on either general or medical literature lack knowledge about medicinal products. In scenarios involving vertical search for products, medicinal product information is typically stored in structured relational databases. Traditional pretrained language models focus on analyzing natural language text in sentences and often overlook the semantic relationships found in structured text within product information tables. 

Considering users' search habits, they not only search for specific medicine names but also frequently look for information related to diseases, symptoms, and other important product attributes. To address this, we propose a new pre-training task called Product Attribute Prediction (PAP). While one approach could involve meticulously constructing a medicinal product knowledge graph from product databases and integrating it into a pre-trained language model, we argue that this process can be labor-intensive and unnecessary. Instead, we advocate for training the language model directly on the structural information of the products for a more efficient method.

title: MultiNERD: A Multilingual, Multi-Genre and Fine-Grained Dataset for Named Entity Recognition (and Disambiguation)
human_text: Since the manual creation of training data for NER is expensive and time-consuming -especially when many languages have to be covered -several studies have tried to address data scarcity by producing training data automatically (Nothman et al., 2013; Al-Rfou et al., 2015; Tsai et al., 2016; Pan et al., 2017) , recently showing that automaticallygenerated annotations can boast a quality comparable to that of manually-created ones (Tedeschi et al., 2021b) . Unfortunately, although these studies have considered a wider range of languages, they have still focused on coarse-grained entities and on a single textual genre, i.e. encyclopedic texts from Wikipedia (Hovy et al., 2013) . In this paper, inspired by the success of the OntoNotes 5.0 dataset and by recent achievements in automatic data creation, we fill the aforementioned gaps and propose the following novel contributions: 1. We design a new language-agnostic methodology for automatically generating high-quality and fine-grained NER annotations by exploiting the texts of Wikipedia and Wikinews foot_1 ; 2. We introduce a novel automatically-created benchmark for NER that covers 10 languages, 15 entity types and 2 textual genres, together with a small manually-curated test set for the English language.
paraphrased_text:
The manual creation of training data for Named Entity Recognition (NER) is a resource-intensive task, particularly when multiple languages need to be considered. To address the challenge of data scarcity, several studies have explored the automatic generation of training data (Nothman et al., 2013; Al-Rfou et al., 2015; Tsai et al., 2016; Pan et al., 2017), demonstrating that automatically generated annotations can achieve a quality comparable to manually created ones (Tedeschi et al., 2021b). However, existing research has primarily focused on coarse-grained entities within a single textual genre, typically encyclopedic texts sourced from Wikipedia (Hovy et al., 2013).

Motivated by the success of the OntoNotes 5.0 dataset and recent advancements in automatic data creation techniques, this paper aims to address the aforementioned limitations by introducing novel contributions. Firstly, we propose a novel language-agnostic methodology for automatically generating high-quality and fine-grained NER annotations by leveraging textual data from both Wikipedia and Wikinews. Secondly, we present a newly created benchmark for NER evaluation that encompasses 10 languages, 15 entity types, and two distinct textual genres. Additionally, we provide a small manually curated test set specifically tailored for the English language.

title: Dictionary-Assisted Supervised Contrastive Learning
human_text: Social science experts often craft these dictionaries, making them useful when the underlying concept of interest is abstract (see, e.g., Brady et al., 2017; Young and Soroka, 2012) . Dictionaries are also useful when specific words that are pivotal to determining the classification of a document may not exist in the training data. This is a particularly salient issue with small corpora, which is often the case in the social sciences. However, recent supervised machine learning approaches do not use these dictionaries. We propose a contrastive learning approach, dictionary-assisted supervised contrastive learning (DASCL), that allows researchers to leverage these expert-crafted dictionaries when fine-tuning pretrained language models.
paraphrased_text: Social science experts often create specialized dictionaries that prove valuable when dealing with abstract concepts (see, for example, Brady et al., 2017; Young and Soroka, 2012). These dictionaries become particularly useful in cases where essential terms crucial for document classification are not present in the training data, a common challenge when working with small corpora in the social sciences. Despite this, current supervised machine learning methods do not incorporate such dictionaries. To address this gap, we introduce a novel approach called Dictionary-Assisted Supervised Contrastive Learning (DASCL), which enables researchers to utilize these expert-curated dictionaries in fine-tuning pre-trained language models.

title: Aspect-Based Emotion Analysis and Multimodal Coreference: A Case Study of Customer Comments on Adidas Instagram Posts
human_text: Given the rise of more visual content, which is illustrated by the popularity of platforms such as Instagram and TikTok, we want to investigate whether text-based models are sufficient to classify social media content which clearly exhibits both textual and visual information in terms of aspect and sentiment/emotion. Moreover, whenever images are presented, text is often used to pinpoint specific aspects of an image (e.g. "Love the color of these"). By closely analysing these instances, and especially those where anaphors are used, we wish to get more insights in whether it would be helpful to include visual information in the ABEA pipeline by means of multimodal coreference resolution. We perform a case study using customer comments on the Adidas Instagram page by collecting 4,900 comments on 175 Instagram images, and annotating them with aspect categories and emotional information. Moreover, the annotators indicated for each comment whether the image was necessary for a full understanding of the comment.
paraphrased_text: With the increasing prevalence of visual content on platforms like Instagram and TikTok, we aim to explore the effectiveness of text-based models in categorizing social media content that includes both text and visuals in terms of aspects and sentiment. Text often complements images by highlighting specific details, such as "Love the color of these." Through a detailed analysis of these instances, particularly those utilizing anaphors, we seek to determine the potential benefits of integrating visual information into the ABEA pipeline through multimodal coreference resolution. To investigate this further, we conducted a case study using 4,900 customer comments on 175 Adidas Instagram images, annotating them with aspect categories and emotional details. Additionally, annotators assessed whether the image was essential for fully understanding each comment.

title: Algorithmic Diversity and Tiny Models: Comparing Binary Networks and the Fruit Fly Algorithm on Document Representation Tasks

Our results show that the two techniques are complementary in strength while both satisfying requirements of energy efficiency. Related work Semantic hashing The task of semantic hashing comes from the area of information retrieval, where it is crucial to be able to cluster documents according to their semantic similarity, in order to retrieve documents given a query. One way of going about this problem is to assign a code, hash or vector representation to each document.
