title: Rewarding Semantic Similarity under Optimized Alignments for {AMR}-to-Text Generation
human_text: While the authors claim greedy alignments have little effect on BERTSCORE evaluation performance, they perform poorly relative to metrics based on optimized alignments in our experiments. Optimized Token Alignment Aligning tokens between hypothesis and reference can be seen as an assignment problem, where a token pair (ŷ i , y j ) is highly weighted if it incurs low cost (i.e., distance). Here, we describe discrete token matching (oneto-one) and soft alignment (one-to-many). For the latter, we extract alignments from the earth mover's distance (EMD, Villani, 2009; Peyré and Cuturi, 2019) transport matrix. We weight pairwise token similarities as in F BERT using each of these two alignments to provide metrics F DISC and F CONT .
paraphrased_text: The authors state that while greedy alignments do not significantly affect the evaluation performance of BERTSCORE, they underperform compared to metrics based on optimized alignments in our experiments. In our study, we explore the concept of optimized token alignment, which involves aligning tokens between the hypothesis and reference as an assignment problem. In this context, a token pair (ŷ i , y j ) is considered highly weighted if it incurs a low cost (i.e., distance). We discuss two approaches to token alignment: discrete token matching (one-to-one) and soft alignment (one-to-many). In the case of soft alignment, we extract alignments from the earth mover's distance (EMD) transport matrix, as described in works by Villani (2009) and Peyré and Cuturi (2019). We calculate pairwise token similarities using these alignments to derive metrics F DISC and F CONT, akin to the approach taken in F BERT.

title: Task-Driven and Experience-Based Question Answering Corpus for In-Home Robot Application in the House3D Virtual Environment
human_text: Assuming two men talk in a house, a default context of their dialog is the surrounding physical environment (their house), which is hardly described by the pure text. Environmental settings have emerged one after another, especially interactive environments for natural language research. Notably, AL-FRED (Action Learning From Realistic Environments and Directives) (Shridhar et al., 2020) , a benchmark for learning a mapping from natural language instructions to sequences of actions for domestic tasks. Naturally, we wonder whether the robot can answer the questions raised by the owner according to the actual situation at home. These questions usually do not have a clear text context (different from the current popular setting of reading comprehension), are directly related to the actual scene, and it is difficult to find the answer from the general knowledge base (such as Wikipedia).
paraphrased_text: When two men engage in conversation inside a house, the default context of their dialogue is often the physical environment around them, which may not be explicitly described in the text. Environmental settings have become increasingly important, particularly in the development of interactive environments for natural language research. One notable example is AL-FRED (Action Learning From Realistic Environments and Directives) (Shridhar et al., 2020), a benchmark designed for learning to interpret natural language instructions and execute corresponding sequences of actions for household tasks. This raises the question of whether a robot can effectively respond to queries posed by the homeowner based on the real-time situation within the home. These queries, unlike typical reading comprehension tasks, lack a clear textual context, are directly tied to the physical environment, and cannot be easily answered using general knowledge databases like Wikipedia.

title: Hate Speech Dynamics Against African descent, Roma and LGBTQ+ Communities in Portugal
human_text: In this paper, hate speech is operationalized through the following coexisting conditions: (i) hate speech has a specific target that can be mentioned explicitly or implicitly in text; (ii) hate speech targets correspond to vulnerable or historically marginalized groups (usually minority groups) or individuals targeted for belonging to those groups; (iii) hate speech typically spreads or supports hatred, or incites violence against the aforementioned targets, by disparaging, humiliating, discriminating, or even threatening them on the basis of specific identity factors; and (iv) hate speech can be expressed both explicitly (or overly) and implicitly (or covertly). The heterogeneity among the existing hate speech corpora is also explained by the diversity of target categories and attributes being considered. While some works are mainly concerned with distinguishing generic categories, such as racism or sexism (Waseem, 2016) , others have adopted complex hierarchical labeling schema, including dozens of hate speech categories and subcategories (Fortuna et al., 2019) . Despite there are several resources and benchmark corpora for many different languages, in particular for English (Poletto et al., 2021) , we have found only four hate speech datasets for Portuguese. 1
paraphrased_text: In this paper, hate speech is defined by several key characteristics. First, hate speech targets a specific group, whether explicitly or implicitly mentioned in text. Typically, these targets are vulnerable or historically marginalized groups, such as minority groups, or individuals within those groups. Second, hate speech often aims to spread or support hatred, inciting violence against these targeted groups through disparaging, humiliating, discriminating, or threatening them based on specific identity factors. Third, hate speech can be expressed both explicitly and implicitly. The variation in hate speech corpora can be attributed to the diversity of target categories and attributes being considered. Some studies focus on generic categories like racism or sexism, while others use complex hierarchical labeling systems with numerous hate speech categories and subcategories. Although there are many hate speech resources and benchmark corpora available for various languages, including English, only four hate speech datasets for Portuguese have been identified.

title: {EAG}: Extract and Generate Multi-way Aligned Corpus for Complete Multi-lingual Neural Machine Translation
human_text: Recently, Freitag and Firat (2020) re-kindle the flame by proposing C-MNMT, which trains the model on the constructed multi-way aligned corpus. Specifically, they extract the multi-way aligned examples by aligning training examples from different language pairs when either their source or target sides are identical (i.e., pivoting through English, for German→English and English→French to extract German-French-English examples). Since they directly extract the multi-way aligned examples from the bilingual corpus, we refer to their approach as the extraction-based approach. Despite improving the performance, the scale of multi-way aligned corpus extracted by Freitag and Firat (2020) is always limited compared to English-centric bilingual corpus, e.g., only 0.3M German-Russian-English multi-way aligned corpus extracted from 4.5M German-English and 33.5M English-Russian bilingual corpus. A simple idea for remedying this problem is to add the roughly-aligned corpus by extracting the training examples when either their source or target sides are highly similar.
paraphrased_text: Freitag and Firat (2020) introduced a new approach called C-MNMT, which involves training a model on a specially constructed multi-way aligned corpus. Their method involves extracting examples that are aligned across different language pairs, using English as a pivot. For example, German→English and English→French training examples are aligned to create German-French-English examples. This method, which we will refer to as the extraction-based approach, has shown improvements in performance. However, the multi-way aligned corpus created by Freitag and Firat (2020) is limited in scale compared to English-centric bilingual corpora. For instance, they extracted only a 0.3M German-Russian-English corpus from a larger pool of bilingual data. To address this limitation, one simple solution is to include roughly-aligned corpus by extracting examples where the source or target sides are highly similar.

title: {SCD}: Self-Contrastive Decorrelation of Sentence Embeddings
human_text: Rather, in order to learn sentence representations, the proposed approach leverages the self -contrast imposed on the augmentations of a single sample. In this regard, the approach builds upon the idea that sufficiently strong perturbation of the sentence embedding reflects the semantic variations of the sentence. However, it is unclear which perturbation is simply a slight variation of the sentence without changing the semantic (positive pair) and which perturbation sufficiently modifies the semantic to create a negative sample. Such ambiguity manifests itself in the augmented sample sharing the characteristics of both negative and positive samples. To accommodate this, we propose an objective function consisting of two opposing terms, which acts on augmentations pairs of a sample: i) self-contrastive divergence (repulsion), and ii) feature decorrelation (attraction).
paraphrased_text: To acquire sentence representations, the proposed methodology exploits self-contrast through augmentations of an individual sample. The approach is grounded in the concept that robust perturbation of the sentence embedding captures semantic nuances within the sentence. Nevertheless, distinguishing between perturbations that introduce only minor changes to the sentence while preserving its semantic essence (positive pairs) and those that significantly alter the semantics to form negative samples remains a challenge. This ambiguity is evident in augmented samples displaying characteristics of both negative and positive pairs. To address this issue, we introduce an objective function comprising two complementary components that operate on pairs of augmentations within a sample: i) self-contrastive divergence (repulsion) and ii) feature decorrelation (attraction).

paraphrased_text: The purpose of these formulas is to adjust the input incrementally towards higher loss values, prompting the model to move in a direction that maximizes loss and leads to an attack. On the other hand, the model must seek robust parameters during optimization to defend against attacks on its samples. By introducing slight perturbations to the embedding features, we mimic the natural errors present in the dataset labels. This strategy nudges the model towards identifying more resilient parameters during training, reducing the influence of aleatoric uncertainty. Consequently, both the model's embedding representation and its overall optimization are enhanced simultaneously.

title: Keyphrase Prediction from Video Transcripts: New Dataset and Directions
human_text: Whereas traditionally feature engineering has been used for KP (Turney, 2000; Sheeba and Vivekanandan, 2014) , recently deep learning is proved to be more efficient for this task (Ye et al., 2021; Ahmad et al., 2021) . However, one limitation in the current works is that they are mainly limited to the formal text such as scientific papers (Meng et al., 2017) and web-logs (Xiong et al., 2019) . As such, the challenges in other domains are still unresolved. Among others, video transcript is one of the less-explored domains that could significantly benefit from KP. For instance, it could be used for video summarization and retrieval or benefit people who are deaf and hard of hearing (DHH) (Kafle et al., 2019) .
paraphrased_text: While traditional approaches have historically relied on feature engineering for keyphrase extraction (Turney, 2000; Sheeba and Vivekanandan, 2014), recent studies have demonstrated the superior efficiency of deep learning methods for this task (Ye et al., 2021; Ahmad et al., 2021). However, a notable limitation of existing research is its predominant focus on formal text genres, such as scientific papers (Meng et al., 2017) and web-logs (Xiong et al., 2019), leaving challenges in other domains unaddressed. Among these domains, video transcripts represent a particularly underexplored area that stands to benefit significantly from keyphrase extraction. Applications in this domain include video summarization, retrieval, and accessibility enhancements for individuals who are deaf or hard of hearing (DHH) (Kafle et al., 2019).

title: Reason first, then respond: Modular Generation for Knowledge-infused Dialogue
human_text: In reinforcement learning settings, generating natural language has been used as an intermediate planning step (Sharma et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2019) , and in particular in goaloriented dialogue (Yarats and Lewis, 2018) and open-domain QA (Adolphs et al., 2021) as well. For summarization tasks, the work of Baziotis et al. (2019) proposes an intermediate autoencoder latent representation. Similarly, West et al. (2019) apply the information bottleneck principle to find an intermediate compressed sentence that can best predict the next sentence. For knowledge-grounded dialogue, an approach using internet search can also be seen as a modular intermediate step, where the search query is first generated (Komeili et al., 2021) .
paraphrased_text: In the field of reinforcement learning, natural language generation serves as a crucial intermediate step for various tasks. This includes goal-oriented dialogue and open-domain question answering. For text summarization, Baziotis and colleagues (2019) introduce an autoencoder latent representation. Similarly, West et al. (2019) employ the information bottleneck principle to create a compressed sentence that predicts the next sentence effectively. In knowledge-grounded dialogue, an approach involving internet search is used as a modular intermediate step. This involves generating a search query first (Komeili et al., 2021).

title: Your fairness may vary: Pretrained language model fairness in toxic text classification
human_text: One aspect of LMs that is hard to ignore is the increase in their size, as measured by the number of parameters in their architectures. In general, larger LMs seem to perform better on NLP tasks as they have the capacity to capture more complex correlations present in the training data. Bender et al. (2021) claim that this same property may also lead to more pronounced biases in their predictions, as the large data that LMs are trained on is not curated. On the other hand, for image classification models that use large neural networks, Hooker et al. (2020) discuss how model pruning can lead to more biased predictions. In this work, we consider a wide variety of model architectures and sizes.
paraphrased_text: One notable aspect of language models (LMs) that cannot be overlooked is their increasing size, which is typically measured by the number of parameters in their structures. Generally, larger LMs tend to outperform smaller ones on natural language processing (NLP) tasks because they possess the capability to capture more intricate correlations found within the training data. According to Bender et al. (2021), this very characteristic may also result in more pronounced biases in their predictions, as the extensive data used to train LMs is not meticulously curated. Conversely, Hooker et al. (2020) explore how model pruning in large neural networks used for image classification can lead to biased predictions. In our study, we examine a diverse range of model architectures and sizes.

title: Threat Scenarios and Best Practices for Neural Fake News Detection
human_text: Dugan et al. (2020) show that humans can easily be fooled when trying to detect the boundary between human and generated text. Their work highlights the difficulty of the detection task, but also shows that humans are capable of identifying generated text in a long document (beyond a couple of sentences). Gehrmann et al. (2019) studied the difference between human and machine generated text. They showed that generated text tends to opt for more common phrases, thus favoring fluency over novelty. They built a tool, GLTR, that helps humans identify generated text, by visualizing the likelihood and rank of words in a text under a language model.
paraphrased_text: Dugan and colleagues (2020) have demonstrated how easily humans can be misled when attempting to distinguish between human and machine-generated text. Their research underscores the challenges of this detection task, while also revealing that humans can successfully identify generated text in longer documents, beyond just a few sentences. In a similar vein, Gehrmann et al. (2019) investigated the distinctions between human and machine-generated text. Their findings indicate that machine-generated text often tends to rely on more commonly used phrases, prioritizing fluency over originality. To aid in this identification process, they developed the GLTR tool, which assists humans by visually representing the likelihood and ranking of words in a text according to a language model.

title: Identification and Analysis of Personification in Hungarian: The PerSECorp project
human_text: Nevertheless, neither the construction-like behavior of personification nor its idiomatic character has been systematically explored yet. Concerning the Hungarian language, the emerging picture of personification is much less grounded in empirical research. According to a comprehensive account of personification as a figure of speech (Sájter, 2008) , the main factors of its description are the following: - the ontology of the personified entity (e.g., abstract things, natural phenomena, physical objects, plants, animals, or groups), - the way of personification (e.g., performing an action, emotion attribution, having a human figure or having mental capabilities), - the grammatical structure of personification (e.g., verbal predicate, possessive construction, nominal and adverbial elements or vocatives), - and the register-specificity of personification (e.g., colloquial, scientific, journalistic, or literal). There are two problems with this comprehensive approach. On the one hand, no empirical investigation supports the proposed factors, rather they are based on professional intuition and a collection of Hungarian personifications (mainly from literal texts) as illustrative examples.
paraphrased_text: Nonetheless, the systematic exploration of both the construction-like nature and idiomatic characteristics of personification remains largely uncharted territory. In the context of the Hungarian language, the scholarly examination of personification is notably lacking in empirical support. A comprehensive study on personification as a rhetorical device (Sájter, 2008) identifies key aspects for its analysis, including the ontological categorization of the personified entity (e.g., abstract concepts, natural elements, physical objects, flora, fauna, or collective entities), the manner in which personification is manifested (e.g., through actions, attribution of emotions, anthropomorphic features, or cognitive attributes), the grammatical structures employed in personification (such as verbal predicates, possessive constructions, nominal and adverbial elements, or vocatives), and the register-specific nuances of personification (ranging from colloquial to scientific or journalistic registers). However, this comprehensive framework faces two significant challenges. Firstly, the proposed factors lack empirical validation, relying instead on expert judgment and a selection of Hungarian personifications drawn primarily from literal texts as illustrative instances.

title: {HOLM}: Hallucinating Objects with Language Models for Referring Expression Recognition in Partially-Observed Scenes
human_text: General knowledge about kitchens can help to know approximately where to look for pans or utensils in a kitchen that has never been seen before. How can an AI system build general knowledge about objects and their environment to help with a similar task? Even more interestingly, can we gather this information from language, using readily available resources such as language models trained on a large collection of unlabeled text? In this paper, we introduce a method called HOLM, Hallucinating Objects with Language Models, for reasoning about the unobserved parts of the environment. Inspired by the recent successes of large pre-trained language models (LM) extracting knowledge about the real world, we propose a methodology based on spatial prompts to extract knowledge from language models about object.
paraphrased_text: A foundational understanding of kitchen layouts can facilitate the location of cookware and utensils within an unfamiliar kitchen environment. How can artificial intelligence (AI) systems acquire broad knowledge about objects and their spatial context to aid in similar tasks? Moreover, is it feasible to derive such knowledge from linguistic sources, leveraging accessible resources like language models trained on extensive corpora of unannotated text? This study introduces a novel approach, termed HOLM (Hallucinating Objects with Language Models), designed to infer information about occluded elements in a given environment. Drawing inspiration from recent advancements in large pre-trained language models' (LM) ability to extract real-world knowledge, we present a methodology centered on spatial prompts for eliciting object-related information from language models.

title: Is More Data Better? Re-thinking the Importance of Efficiency in Abusive Language Detection with Transformers-Based Active Learning
human_text: Our first subquestion asks, RQ1.1: What effect do model pre-training and architecture have on efficiency and effectiveness? To answer RQ1.1, we evaluate transformers-and traditional-based AL in a simulated setup using two already-labeled abusive language datasets. One challenge in abusive language detection is class imbalance as, although extremely harmful, abuse comprises a small portion of online content (Vidgen et al., 2019) . Prior AL work primarily uses datasets at their given class imbalances and thus has not disentangled how class imbalance versus linguistic features affect the design choices needed for efficient AL. This is a problem given that most abusive language datasets do not reflect the imbalance actually observed in the wild.
paraphrased_text: Our first subquestion, RQ1.1, explores the impact of model pre-training and architecture on the efficiency and effectiveness of abusive language detection. To address RQ1.1, we compare transformers and traditional models in an artificial environment using two pre-labeled datasets containing abusive language. One significant challenge in detecting abusive language is the imbalance in class distribution, as abusive content constitutes a small fraction of online content (Vidgen et al., 2019). Previous active learning (AL) research has typically used datasets with their inherent class imbalances, making it difficult to separate the effects of class imbalance and linguistic features on the optimal design choices for efficient AL. This is problematic because real-world abusive language datasets often do not accurately reflect the observed class imbalance.

human_text: In this paper, we propose a novel attentionbased, regularized user-aware modeling approach for detecting disruptive talk in multi-party dialogue within a collaborative game-based learning environment. We investigate the use of target-user embeddings to help the prediction model determine the disruptiveness of the sequence more accurately with an additional user-specific network and attention mechanism. We also investigate a sequence-level dropout mechanism during training as a regularization technique that could help avoid overfitting possible diluted conversation sequences (i.e., presence of multiple threads in a sequence) in training data. Experimental results demonstrate that our attention-based, regularized user-aware model offers great potential for addressing disruptive talk detection in multi-party dialogues. Related Work Diverse prediction tasks have analyzed multi-party dialogue focusing on the asynchronous and entangled nature of group conversations, such as dialogue act classification using group thread history, and thread detection as well as cyberbullying and toxic message detection within group conversations (Anikina and Kruijff-Korbayova, 2019;
paraphrased_text: In this study, we propose a new method for identifying disruptive speech in group conversations during collaborative gaming in a learning setting. Our approach involves using attention-based modeling that is tailored to individual users and includes regularization techniques. By incorporating user-specific embeddings and attention mechanisms, our model can better predict disruptive behavior in conversation sequences. We also employ sequence-level dropout during training to prevent overfitting on complex conversation threads. Our experimental results show that this user-aware, attention-based model has the potential to effectively detect disruptive speech in group discussions. Previous research has explored various prediction tasks in multi-party dialogues, including analyzing dialogue acts, identifying conversation threads, and detecting cyberbullying and toxic messages in group conversations.

title: On Transferability of Prompt Tuning for Natural Language Processing
human_text: Although PT is an effective approach to utilizing extremely large PLMs, it requires much more training time than fine-tuning to reach the convergence as shown in Figure 2 ; hence, it is worthwhile to explore how to improve the efficiency of PT. In this work, we attempt to improve PT via prompt transfer across different tasks and models. Knowledge transfer across tasks (Vu et al., 2020) and models (Qin et al., 2021) have been widely used to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of NLP systems. Intuitively, soft prompts are the only tuned parameters in PT and thus shall concentrate the knowledge required to solve tasks conditioned on PLMs. Thus transferring the trained prompts is promising to accelerate PT.
paraphrased_text: Although prompt tuning (PT) is an effective approach for utilizing extremely large pre-trained language models (PLMs), it requires significantly more computational resources and training time than fine-tuning to achieve convergence, as shown in Figure 2. Therefore, it is worth exploring ways to enhance the efficiency of PT. In this study, we aim to enhance PT through prompt transfer across various tasks and models. The transfer of knowledge across tasks (Vu et al., 2020) and models (Qin et al., 2021) has been widely employed to boost the efficiency and efficacy of natural language processing (NLP) systems. Given that soft prompts are the only parameters fine-tuned in PT, they encapsulate the essential knowledge needed to address tasks based on PLMs. Consequently, transferring the trained prompts holds promise for expediting PT.

title: Efficiently and Thoroughly Anonymizing a Transformer Language Model for Dutch Electronic Health Records: a Two-Step Method

In Section 6, we will conclude, discussing some limitations and considerations for further research. MedRoBERTa.nl MedRoBERTa.nl (Verkijk and Vossen, 2022) is the first domain specific LM for the free text contained in Dutch Electronic Health Records (EHRs). title: Response Construct Tagging: {NLP}-Aided Assessment for Engineering Education
human_text: Entry and exit surveys measure whether students undergo a transformative experience (Pugh, 2002) in the course, and assess the impact of the course on their engineering identity (Stevens et al., 2008) . These aspects are characterized by six specific constructs, listed in Table 1 . We introduce a new task, response construct tagging (RCT), in which the goal is to identify student affect towards all six constructs from an open-ended response. For example, if a student response says "I'm not sure what specific career I will pursue, but as long as it's engineering, I'm fine with it. ", then, they are displaying a positive affect towards the Identification construct since they see themselves as an engineer.
paraphrased_text: Entry and exit surveys are used to evaluate whether students undergo a transformative experience during a course and to determine the course's impact on their engineering identity. These surveys are based on six specific constructs, which are detailed in Table 1. A new method called response construct tagging (RCT) has been introduced to identify student attitudes towards these constructs in open-ended responses. For instance, if a student's response indicates, "I am uncertain about my specific career path, but as long as it involves engineering, I am content," they are demonstrating a positive attitude towards the Identification construct by identifying themselves as an engineer.

title: Inferring Ranked Dialog Flows from Human-to-Human Conversations
human_text: Nowadays there exists a wide range of platforms for implementing virtual assistants, such as Google DialogFlow, foot_0 Amazon Lex, foot_1 Microsoft Bot Framework, 3 and RASA. 4 However, implementing a virtual assistant or extending it to support new services is not a trivial task. For the case of new services, one has to imagine how the conversations for that given service will be, or run a Wizard of Oz experiment with potential customers to gather examples of conversations. Once a conversational agent is deployed, it is often necessary to review its performance and adapt it to the actual conversations. For the case of services that are already being provided by human agents (e.g. in a call center), it is possible to review the conversation recordings in order to design a virtual assistant that will be better suited when first deployed.
paraphrased_text: Nowadays, there is a wide range of platforms available for implementing virtual assistants, such as Google DialogFlow, Amazon Lex, Microsoft Bot Framework, and RASA. However, developing a virtual assistant or expanding its capabilities to support new services is not a simple task. When considering new services, one must envision how conversations for that particular service will flow, or conduct a Wizard of Oz experiment with potential customers to collect sample conversations. After deploying a conversational agent, it is often necessary to evaluate its performance and make adjustments based on real-world interactions. In cases where services are already handled by human agents (e.g., in a call center), reviewing conversation recordings can help in designing a more effective virtual assistant before its initial deployment.

title: Does {BERT} Know that the {IS}-A Relation Is Transitive?
human_text: The pre-trained language model BERT has been extensively analyzed since its release. In particular, feature-based probes have been proposed to show how a particular layer, head, or neuron of BERT works on a downstream NLP task. Usually with a small set of additional parameters, a probe is trained in a supervised manner using feature representations from the pre-trained BERT, e.g., contextualized embeddings, to solve a particular task (Wu et al., 2020) . Attention and structural probes have been invented to investigate different aspects of BERT and linguistic properties (Lin et al., 2019; Jawahar et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2019; Hewitt and Manning, 2019; Manning et al., 2020; Tenney et al., 2019a,b; Pruksachatkun et al., 2020) . Latent ontology of contextual embeddings has also been investigated via cluster analysis (Michael et al., 2020) .
paraphrased_text: The pre-trained language model Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) has undergone extensive scrutiny following its initial release. Notably, feature-based probes have been introduced to elucidate the functioning of specific layers, heads, or neurons within BERT in the context of downstream Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks. These probes are typically trained in a supervised manner with a limited number of additional parameters, utilizing feature representations derived from the pre-trained BERT, such as contextualized embeddings, to address specific tasks (Wu et al., 2020). Attention and structural probes have been developed to explore various facets of BERT and linguistic characteristics (Lin et al., 2019; Jawahar et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2019; Hewitt and Manning, 2019; Manning et al., 2020; Tenney et al., 2019a,b; Pruksachatkun et al., 2020). Furthermore, research has delved into the latent ontology of contextual embeddings through cluster analysis (Michael et al., 2020).

title: Reducing Conversational Agents' Overconfidence Through Linguistic Calibration

This goal applies Grice's maxim of quality (Grice, 1975) on a metacognitive level, namely, being truthful about what one knows. Here, this would mean that if we can train accurate predictors of correctness from information available to the model (input words and internal representations), then model generations should convey that information. The skill of handling uncertainty would be desirable even if accuracy on factual questions ever became perfect: Some questions do not have known answers, or have answers that depend on a context that a dialogue agent cannot know, making it perilous to ''ignore ignorance" (Smithson, 2012; Ravetz, 1993) .
paraphrased_text: This approach promotes transparency within models by explicitly acknowledging their limitations during interactions, rather than relying on external instructions that users may disregard or forget. This objective aligns with Grice's maxim of quality (Grice, 1975) at a metacognitive level, emphasizing the importance of honesty regarding one's knowledge. In practice, this entails training models to accurately predict the correctness of responses based on available information such as input words and internal representations, and subsequently incorporating this information into the generated responses. Proficiency in handling uncertainty remains crucial even when factual accuracy reaches high levels, as some questions may lack definitive answers or be context-dependent, highlighting the risks of overlooking gaps in knowledge (Smithson, 2012; Ravetz, 1993).

title: Topology imbalance and Relation inauthenticity aware Hierarchical Graph Attention Networks for Fake News Detection
human_text: However, in many cases, the relationships in NPG can be manipulated by users and thus are unauthentic. This leads to inaccurate NPG with unauthentic propagation structure, which confuses existing social context-based methods to make an incorrect prediction. Besides, NPG usually has the problem of topology imbalance: the imbalance caused by the asymmetric and uneven topology of labeled nodes, where the decision boundaries are driven by the labeled nodes close to the topological class boundaries thus interfering with the model learning (Chen et al., 2021) . Ideally, the influence from labeled nodes should decay with the topology distance and also the node influence boundaries should be consistent with the true class boundaries.
paraphrased_text: However, many relationships in Network Propagation Graphs (NPG) can be manipulated by users, resulting in inauthentic interactions. For instance, fake news creators may influence other users to post fake comments supporting their misinformation or delete genuine comments that challenge it. This manipulation creates a misleading NPG structure that can confuse social context-based methods and lead to inaccurate predictions. Additionally, NPG often suffers from topology imbalance, which stems from the uneven distribution and asymmetry of labeled nodes. This imbalance can affect decision boundaries, particularly when labeled nodes near class boundaries influence model learning. Ideally, the influence of labeled nodes should diminish with topological distance, and node influence boundaries should align with true class boundaries to improve model accuracy.

They may approach the problem from different angles by considering different equivalence relations within the problem. As depicted in the top part of Figure 1, there are at least two distinct ways to solve this problem using different reasoning strategies: On the left side, one can set up equations based on the equivalence relation between the total calories of the cheeseburger and pizza; whereas on the right side, another approach involves focusing first on the equivalence relation of the cheeseburger's caloric content and adjusting the pizza's calories accordingly. These diverse reasoning strategies can result in different sets of equations, leading to mathematically equivalent forms of the solution, as illustrated by expressions 1 and 2 in the example. However, a longstanding limitation in previous studies is that they often impose a fixed equation structure determined by human input, restricting the solver's options.

title: AnEMIC: A Framework for Benchmarking ICD Coding Models

For instance, the label set of MIMIC-III top-50 dataset varies among the literature, and some of them are even used incorrectly. paraphrased_text: The absence of a standardized benchmark poses a significant challenge for advancing research in the field of Natural Language Processing (NLP). These inconsistencies in dataset processing and the errors they introduce hinder the comparability of different methodologies. This paper presents a novel framework for benchmarking automatic ICD coding using the MIMIC clinical database.

For another thing, though the two techniques seem to be orthogonal and probably complementary to each other, it remains a potential challenge to effectively and naturally integrate them. Although these techniques may appear independent and potentially complementary, effectively and seamlessly integrating them poses a challenge. In this study, we revisit token substitution and mixup methods for Named Entity Recognition (NER) and explore these distinct techniques within a unified meta reweighting framework, as depicted in Figure 1. Our approach aims to broaden the scope of these methods, allowing for a more diverse and extensive set of pseudo training examples by relaxing previous constraints.

title: Taking Actions Separately: A Bidirectionally-Adaptive Transfer Learning Method for Low-Resource Neural Machine Translation
human_text: Different from the previous work, we neither use an unabridged Parent encoder for spaceshareable encoding, nor directly transfer it towards low-resource translation. Instead, we divide the unabridged Parent encoder into two parts. One part engages in space-shareable encoding for alleviating cold-start fine-tuning problems. The other is straight transferred without being "brainwashed" for the pre-existing translation experiences (i.e., the ones learned during high-resource NMT). The goal is to fulfill bidirectional adaptation, i.e., 1) establishing the encoding mode that adapts to linguistic characteristics of low-resource languages, conditioned on the shareable latent space; and 2) preserving the encoding mode that adapts to original translation experiences of the Parent NMT model.
paraphrased_text: In contrast to prior research, our approach involves a novel methodology that diverges from utilizing an unaltered Parent encoder for space-shareable encoding or direct application in low-resource translation tasks. Instead, we propose a bifurcation of the unaltered Parent encoder into two distinct components. One component is dedicated to space-shareable encoding to address challenges related to cold-start fine-tuning, while the other component is transferred without modification to maintain the existing translation capabilities acquired during high-resource Neural Machine Translation (NMT). This dual-component framework aims to achieve bidirectional adaptation by: 1) establishing an encoding mechanism that adjusts to the linguistic attributes of low-resource languages within the context of a shareable latent space; and 2) retaining the original encoding mechanism that reflects the translation experiences of the Parent NMT model.

title: Distantly Supervised Named Entity Recognition via Confidence-Based Multi-Class Positive and Unlabeled Learning

Furthermore, the PU learning theory is built on a fundamental assumption of data distribution that unlabeled data can accurately reveal the overall distribution (i.e., the marginal distribution of the target field) (Bekker and Davis, 2020) . paraphrased_text: Firstly, using multiple binary classifiers may not be efficient, especially when dealing with numerous entity types in a Named Entity Recognition (NER) task. Each entity type requires its own classifier to be trained. Secondly, there may be discrepancies in the scale of predicted confidence values across these classifiers, which could impact the final prediction quality (Bishop, 2006). Additionally, the Positive and Unlabeled (PU) learning theory is founded on the assumption that unlabeled data accurately reflects the overall data distribution, including the target field's marginal distribution (Bekker and Davis, 2020). However, in Distantly Supervised Named Entity Recognition (DS-NER) tasks, the training data annotated with distant supervision may not align well with this assumption. Its efficacy relies on the coverage of dictionaries or knowledge bases used for entity identification.

title: SLICER: Sliced Fine-Tuning for Low-Resource Cross-Lingual Transfer for Named Entity Recognition
human_text: In the higher layers -parameters of which change the most through NER fine-tuning -tokens start placing much more attention to themselves than in the vanilla XLM-R. The gap is particularly pronounced from the 9th layer onwards and amounts to roughly 10% more attending-to-self in the last layer, compared to the pretrained XLM-R. This suggests that monolingual NER favors (or, more precisely, requires) reduced contextualization in higher Transformer layers. This effectively means that the Transformer places more focus on token information itself, which, we hypothesize, leads to more similar representations for tokens with similar properties, regardless of their context. In monolingual NER, this is arguably beneficial because, within the same language, NE tokens generally share many token-level properties (e.g., morphosyntax and capitalization). Because of this, the same decontextualization effect, we argue, should have a detrimental effect in cross-lingual transfer to target languages in which NE tokens generally do not share token-level properties with NE tokens of the source language. Sliced Fine-Tuning for Cross-Lingual NER.
paraphrased_text: In the upper layers, where the parameters change the most during Named Entity Recognition (NER) fine-tuning, tokens begin to pay more attention to themselves compared to the original XLM-R model. This shift becomes especially noticeable starting from the 9th layer, with approximately 10% more self-attention in the final layer compared to the pretrained XLM-R model. This observation indicates that in monolingual NER tasks, there is a preference (or necessity) for reduced contextualization in the higher Transformer layers. Essentially, this means that the Transformer model focuses more on the individual token information, resulting in similar representations for tokens with similar properties, regardless of their context. This focus is advantageous in monolingual NER as named entity tokens within the same language often share common token-level characteristics such as morphosyntax and capitalization. However, this decontextualization effect could hinder cross-lingual transfer to target languages where named entities do not share the same token-level properties as in the source language. This concept is explored in the context of Sliced Fine-Tuning for Cross-Lingual NER.

title: Hero-Gang Neural Model For Named Entity Recognition
human_text: Method NER is usually performed as a sequence labeling problem. As summarized in Figure 1 , the Transformer-based models (e.g., BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) , XLNET (Yang et al., 2019b) ) are regarded as the Hero module to model the entire sentence for global sequence information extraction and the Gang module is responsible for local and relative position information extraction. Afterward, we employ the multi-window attention to elaborately combine these different features (i.e., features extracted from the Hero and Gang modules), which is then used to predict labels for each token. Therefore, the aforementioned process can be formulated as: Ŷ = f (X, H(X), G(X)), (1) where H(•) and G(•) refer to the Hero and Gang modules, respectively, and the details of them are presented in the following subsections.
paraphrased_text: Named Entity Recognition (NER) is commonly approached as a sequence labeling task. Specifically, given a sequence of tokens X = x1, x2, ..., xN with N tokens, the goal is to train a model that assigns labels Ŷ = ŷ1, ŷ2, ŷ3, ..., ŷN to each token in the sequence. Transformer-based models, such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) and XLNET (Yang et al., 2019b), are considered the core components for capturing global sequence information (referred to as the Hero module), while the local and relative position information is extracted by the Gang module, as illustrated in Figure 1. The combination of features from these modules, achieved through multi-window attention, is then utilized to predict labels for each token. This process is captured by the formula Ŷ = f(X, H(X), G(X)), where H(•) and G(•) represent the Hero and Gang modules, respectively. Further details about these modules are provided in the subsequent sections.

title: {U}ni{GDD}: {A} Unified Generative Framework for Goal-Oriented Document-Grounded Dialogue
human_text: Although KI and RG can be unified with the proposed generative method, they have different characteristics. Generating the grounding knowledge is similar to copying appropriate sentences from the document, while generating the response needs more effort to make the response coherent with the dialogue and consistent with the grounding knowledge. Therefore, in addition to the main task that uses the concatenation of the grounding knowledge and response as the target sequence, we introduce the generation of the grounding knowledge and the generation of the response as two auxiliary tasks in the same framework to force the model to capture their characteristics so as to perform well on them as well. Moreover, inspired by the recent success in prompt learning for pre-trained models (Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021) , we design prompts for these three tasks to guide the model on what to generate for each task. These prompts can naturally connect these tasks via indicating the model that each auxiliary task aims to generate a part of the target sequence of the main task.
paraphrased_text: Although Knowledge Integration (KI) and Response Generation (RG) can be integrated using the proposed generative method, they exhibit distinct characteristics. The process of generating grounding knowledge resembles extracting relevant sentences from the source document, whereas generating responses entails greater complexity in ensuring coherence with the dialogue and alignment with the grounding knowledge. Consequently, in addition to the primary objective of concatenating grounding knowledge and response as the target sequence, we advocate for the incorporation of grounding knowledge generation and response generation as auxiliary tasks within the same framework. This approach compels the model to adeptly capture the unique attributes of each task, thereby enhancing performance across all components. Furthermore, drawing inspiration from recent advancements in prompt-based learning for pre-trained models (Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021), we formulate prompts tailored to guide the model in executing these three tasks effectively. By delineating specific instructions for each task, these prompts establish a cohesive link between the auxiliary tasks and the main objective, guiding the model in generating segments that collectively contribute to the target sequence.

title: A sequence-to-sequence approach for document-level relation extraction
human_text: Task definition: document-level relation extraction: Given a source document of S tokens, a model must extract all tuples corresponding to a relation, R, expressed between the entities, E in the document, (E 1 , ..., E n , R) where n is the number of participating entities, or arity, of the relation. Each entity E i is represented as the set of its coreferent mentions {e i j } in the document, which are often expressed as aliases, abbreviations or acronyms. All entities appearing in a tuple have at least one mention in the document. The mentions that express a given relation are not necessarily contained within the same sentence. Commonly, E is assumed to be known and provided as input to a model.
paraphrased_text: Task Description: In document-level relation extraction, a model needs to identify all tuples representing a specific relationship (R) between entities (E) in a source document with S tokens. Each tuple consists of n participating entities (E1, ..., En) and the relation R. The entities (Ei) are represented by a set of their coreferent mentions {eij} in the document, which may include aliases, abbreviations, or acronyms. Every entity mentioned in a tuple must have at least one mention in the document. It is not necessary for mentions of entities involved in the same relation to appear in the same sentence. Typically, the entities (E) are assumed to be known and provided as input to the model.

title: HateCheckHIn: Evaluating Hindi Hate Speech Detection Models
human_text: Software engineering research has many paradigms and tools for testing complex software systems. In particular, "functional testing" (a type of black-box testing) involves examining the various capabilities of a system by assessing input-output behavior without any knowledge of the internal working mechanism of the system. In recent times, researchers have started applying the knowledge of the software engineering domain to NLP models to measure the robustness of such models. Recently, Röttger et al. (2020) introduced HATE-CHECK foot_0 , a suite of functional tests to measure the quality of hate speech detection models in English. HATECHECK covers 29 model functionalities among which 18 correspond to distinct expressions of hate and the rest 11 are non-hateful contrasts to the hateful cases.
paraphrased_text: Software engineering research encompasses a variety of paradigms and tools designed to test complex software systems. One notable approach is "functional testing," a form of black-box testing that involves evaluating a system's capabilities by analyzing its input-output behavior without requiring knowledge of its internal workings. Recently, researchers have begun leveraging insights from the software engineering field to assess the robustness of natural language processing (NLP) models. For instance, Röttger et al. (2020) introduced HATE-CHECK foot_0, a comprehensive suite of functional tests aimed at evaluating the performance of hate speech detection models in English. HATE-CHECK comprises 29 model functionalities, including 18 distinct expressions of hate and 11 non-hateful examples that serve as contrasts to the hateful instances.

title: Overcoming a Theoretical Limitation of Self-Attention

Additionally, incorporating a new entity necessitates modifying the index itself. To tackle these challenges, De Cao et al. Recent research has also introduced the incorporation of semantic consistency into consistency regularization methods for training LMs, aiming to enhance the models' inductive bias (Wang and Henao, 2021; Zheng et al., 2021; Kim et al., 2021).

title: {N}oisy{T}une: A Little Noise Can Help You Finetune Pretrained Language Models Better
human_text: We conduct extensive experiments on two widely used NLP benchmarks, namely, GLUE (Wang et al., 2018) for English language understanding and XTREME (Hu et al., 2020) for multilingual language understanding. The results show NoisyTune can empower the finetuning of different PLMs on many different downstream NLP tasks to consistently achieve better performance. In addition, the results show NoisyTune can be easily combined with many existing PLM finetuning methods and further improve their performance. The goal of NoisyTune is for more effective finetuning of PLMs on downstream tasks. The motivation of NoisyTune is that PLMs are well pretrained on some unlabeled corpus with some self-supervision tasks, and they may overfit these pretraining data and tasks (Qi et al., 2020) , which usually have gap with the downstream task and data.
paraphrased_text: We conducted thorough experiments on two prominent Natural Language Processing (NLP) benchmarks: the General Language Understanding Evaluation (GLUE) dataset (Wang et al., 2018) for English language comprehension and the Cross-lingual TRansfer Evaluation of Multilingual Encoders (XTREME) benchmark (Hu et al., 2020) for multilingual language understanding. Our findings demonstrate that NoisyTune significantly enhances the fine-tuning process of various Pretrained Language Models (PLMs) across diverse downstream NLP tasks, consistently yielding superior performance. Furthermore, our results indicate that NoisyTune can be seamlessly integrated with numerous existing PLM fine-tuning techniques, leading to further performance enhancements. The primary objective of NoisyTune is to facilitate more efficient fine-tuning of PLMs for downstream tasks. The rationale behind the development of NoisyTune lies in the observation that PLMs, which are pretrained on unlabeled corpora using self-supervised tasks, may exhibit overfitting to the pretraining data and tasks (Qi et al., 2020). This phenomenon often results in a misalignment between the pretraining objectives and the requirements of downstream tasks and datasets.

title: Exploring Mode Connectivity for Pre-trained Language Models
human_text: In the experiments, we observe that for two minima obtained independently on two tasks, when traversing from the minima trained on a source task to that of a target task, a PLM suffers from catastrophic forgetting (McCloskey and Cohen, 1989) on the source task, and gradually absorbs the knowledge from the target task. Besides, PLMs prioritize forgetting those elusive source knowledge and acquiring easy-to-grasp target knowledge. In general, to fathom the connection of minima reached under different settings, we conduct empirical studies on the mode connectivity of PLMs. We also show that our findings may have potential significance in broad research areas, such as designing better ensemble methods for PLMs, understanding the task-level transferability of PLMs and revealing the mechanism of PLM downstream adaptation. We expect our evaluation setup and findings could inspire more future works in this field.
paraphrased_text: In our experiments, we have observed that when moving from a minimum trained on a source task to one from a target task, a pre-trained language model (PLM) experiences catastrophic forgetting on the source task but gradually incorporates knowledge from the target task. The PLM tends to prioritize forgetting complex source knowledge in favor of acquiring easier-to-understand target knowledge. To better understand the relationship between minima obtained under different conditions, we have conducted empirical studies on the connectivity of PLMs. Our research also suggests that these findings could have implications for various areas, including the development of improved ensemble methods for PLMs, assessing the transferability of PLMs across tasks, and understanding how PLMs adapt downstream. We hope that our evaluation framework and results will inspire further research in this field.

title: {BMI}nf: An Efficient Toolkit for Big Model Inference and Tuning
human_text: However, the inference and tuning process of PLMs depends on specific application scenarios and will frequently use big models for computation. If we still stack devices to speed up the inference and tuning of big models, the cost of time, memory, and even money would become unbearable. In this paper, we introduce a toolkit BMInf, aiming at efficiently performing big model inference and tuning. As shown in Figure 1 , BMInf is built based on a four-level framework, the most important part of which lies in its algorithm level and implementation level. At the algorithm level, we introduce model quantization to compress big models from high-bit floating-point parameters to low-bit fixed-point ones, which can significantly reduce the memory cost of big models.
paraphrased_text: The inference and tuning process of Pre-trained Language Models (PLMs) relies on specific application contexts and often involves the utilization of large models for computational tasks. Employing multiple devices to expedite the inference and tuning of large models can lead to considerable costs in terms of time, memory, and financial resources. This paper presents a toolkit called BMInf that aims to facilitate efficient execution of inference and tuning tasks for large models. Illustrated in Figure 1, BMInf is constructed upon a four-tier framework, with a pivotal focus on its algorithmic and implementation components. At the algorithmic level, model quantization is introduced as a means to compress large models by converting high-precision floating-point parameters to lower-precision fixed-point ones, thereby substantially reducing the memory overhead associated with large models.

title: Does {BERT} really agree ? Fine-grained Analysis of Lexical Dependence on a Syntactic Task
human_text: To answer this question, we test BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) against the NA task while controlling both the syntactic constructions and the meaningfulness of the stimuli presented to the model. Our experiments provide two main findings. Contrarily to previous observations that BERT performs fairly well on Gulordava et al.'s (2018) syntactically well-formed but meaningless sentences (Goldberg, 2019) , we show that its generalization abilities are not lexically-independent on syntactic constructions where an attractor is present. Though the model has been previously shown to ignore attractors belonging to an embedded clause independent of that containing the target (Goldberg, 2019), we further provide insights on this lexical dependence that reveal the limitations of the model's abilities. Our experiments rather show that the model is actually sensitive to the presence of attractors when semantic and lexical patterns are disrupted in its input sentence.

Our empirical investigations yield two key findings. In contrast to previous observations suggesting BERT's proficient performance on syntactically coherent yet semantically vacuous sentences as delineated by Gulordava et al. (2018) and corroborated by Goldberg (2019), our study reveals that the model's capacity for generalization is not entirely independent of lexical considerations in syntactic constructions featuring attractors. Notably, while prior research has demonstrated BERT's ability to disregard attractors within an embedded clause distinct from the target clause (Goldberg, 2019), our study sheds new light on the model's lexical sensitivity, highlighting its limitations in handling certain syntactic configurations. Specifically, our findings indicate that the model exhibits a heightened sensitivity to attractors when faced with disruptions in both semantic and lexical patterns within the input sentence.

title: Learn from Relation Information: Towards Prototype Representation Rectification for Few-Shot Relation Extraction
human_text: Most RE models (Distiawan et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019; Jin et al., 2020) require large labeled datasets while constructing such datasets is usually high-costing and timeconsuming. Thus, the Few-shot Relation Extraction (FSRE) has become a hot topic to alleviate data scarcity. There are two main steps in FSRE. The model is first trained on collections of fewshot tasks (i.e., meta tasks) sampled from the largescale data containing disjoint relations and then fast adapted to the unseen relation classes with few samples. One of the popular algorithms is the Prototype Network (Snell et al., 2017) , which is based on the meta-learning framework (Vilalta and Drissi, 2002; Vanschoren, 2018) , and the basic framework used in the paper.
paraphrased_text: Numerous relation extraction (RE) models, as discussed by Distiawan et al. (2019), Li et al. (2019), and Jin et al. (2020), typically rely on extensive labeled datasets. However, the creation of such datasets is often resource-intensive and time-consuming. Consequently, the concept of Few-shot Relation Extraction (FSRE) has emerged as a prominent area of research to address the challenge of limited data availability. FSRE involves a two-fold approach: initially training the model on a set of few-shot tasks (referred to as meta tasks) extracted from large-scale datasets containing distinct relations, followed by rapid adaptation to novel relation classes with minimal samples. Notably, the Prototype Network algorithm (Snell et al., 2017) is widely recognized in this context, leveraging the meta-learning framework (Vilalta and Drissi, 2002; Vanschoren, 2018) as the foundational basis for the methodologies discussed in this study.

title: Visual Named Entity Linking: A New Dataset and A Baseline
human_text: Given an image without textual description, the goal is to link the visual mention in the image with the whole image as the context to the corresponding named entity in KBs. Considering the format of entity in KBs, such as textual descriptions, images, and other structured attributes, we further introduce three sub-tasks according to the type of entity context, i.e., the visual to visual entity linking (V2VEL), visual to textual entity linking (V2TEL), and visual to visual-textual entity linking (V2VTEL). We believe these tasks could put forward higher requirements and more detailed granularity for image understanding, crossmodal alignment, and multi-modal fusion. Following the definition of VNEL, currently public available EL datasets may not fit for our research, as they either only focus on textual modality or lack of detailed annotations for entity information in each image. As a result, we release a new dataset called WIKIPerson.
paraphrased_text: The objective is to connect a visual reference within an image to the corresponding entity in knowledge bases (KBs) using the image as the context. To align with the different formats of entities in KBs, such as textual descriptions, images, and structured attributes, we have defined three sub-tasks based on the type of entity context: visual to visual entity linking (V2VEL), visual to textual entity linking (V2TEL), and visual to visual-textual entity linking (V2VTEL). These tasks aim to enhance image understanding, crossmodal alignment, and multi-modal fusion by requiring a more detailed analysis. However, existing publicly available entity linking datasets do not fully meet our requirements as they are focused solely on textual modality or lack detailed annotations for entity information in images. Therefore, we have introduced a new dataset called WIKIPerson to address this gap.

human_text: We suspect that low performance stems from the fact that LMs are struggling to encode world knowledge, which is often required in NLI and logic datasets (Clark et al., 2007; Wang et al., 2018; Lauscher et al., 2020; Kassner et al., 2020; Ryb and Van Schijndel, 2021) , while high performance may be due to extracting overlapping heuristics (Beall et al., 2019; McCoy et al., 2019) , or to attending to shallow predictors such as the presence of specific words or sentence grammaticality (Poliak et al., 2018) . We control for these factors in AnaLog. Probing typically consists of extracting model representations, feeding them as input to a supervised classifier trained to predict a hypothesised linguistic property (e.g., the grammatical number agreement of the main verb of a sentence), and testing the probing classifier on a set of unseen representations. Good probing performance cannot directly be taken to indicate that the hypothesised linguistic property is tracked by the LM (Belinkov, 2021) . It is thus common practice to compare the true probing performance of classifiers with performance on control representations (Zhang and Bowman, 2018; Tenney et al., 2018; Chrupała et al., 2020) , tasks (Hewitt and Liang, 2019a) , or datasets (Ravichander et al., 2021) .
paraphrased_text:
We believe that the low performance of language models (LMs) may be because they struggle to encode world knowledge, which is often necessary for tasks like Natural Language Inference (NLI) and logic datasets. On the other hand, high performance could be attributed to the models extracting common patterns or focusing on simple cues like specific words or sentence structure. In our work, AnaLog, we address and control for these factors.

Probing involves extracting model representations, using them as input for a supervised classifier trained to predict a linguistic property (e.g., subject-verb agreement), and then evaluating the classifier on new representations. However, good probing performance does not necessarily prove that the LM is tracking the hypothesized linguistic property. To address this, it is common practice to compare the probing performance with performance on control representations, tasks, or datasets.

title: On Decoding and Discourse Structure in Multi-Modal Text Generation
human_text: On the importance of decoding It is impossible to neglect the impact of the choice of the decoding on the structure of the generated texts. Discourse in multi-modal descriptions can be affected by many factors, including scene structure (Linde and Goguen, 1980) , the desire to have more accurate or more diverse texts (Massarelli et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021) and aspects of the task (Kiddon et al., 2016; Narayan et al., 2022) . Other constraints include adherence to a specific topic as in poetry generation by controlling for content and form (Hopkins and Kiela, 2017) and incorporating pragmatic reasoning when describing images with text (Cohn-Gordon et al., 2018; Vedantam et al., 2017) or optimising model's predictions for a specific metric (Rennie et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2017; Zarrieß and Schlangen, 2018) in the spirit of reinforcement learning.
paraphrased_text: Discussing the importance of decoding is crucial, as the choice of decoding greatly influences the structure of the resulting texts. Various factors can impact discourse in multi-modal descriptions, such as scene structure, the goal of achieving more accurate or diverse texts, and specific task requirements. Additional constraints may involve staying on a particular topic, like in poetry generation where content and form are carefully controlled, or considering pragmatic reasoning when describing images with text. Furthermore, models may be optimized for specific metrics through reinforcement learning approaches.

title: Bidimensional Leaderboards: Generate and Evaluate Language Hand in Hand
human_text: They were adopted based on reported correlations with human judgments at the time the metrics were introduced, but it has since been established that the correspondence can collapse when models of different types are compared (Callison-Burch et al., 2006) or models become increasingly powerful (Ma et al., 2019; Edunov et al., 2020) . Meanwhile, many evaluation metrics that improve correlation with human judgments have been proposed (Clark et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020b; Sellam et al., 2020; Hessel et al., 2021, inter alia) , but this progress has yet to be broadly adopted by the community of researchers focused on advancing models. Indeed, consistent with prior metaevaluations (Marie et al., 2021) , we found that 68% of the machine translation papers from NAACL and ACL 2021 evaluated their models solely by BLEU, and only 5% measured the performance using recent metrics with contextual representations such as COMET (Rei et al., 2020) . Similarly, automatic evaluation in 66% of the summarization papers was done only in terms of ROUGE. 2
paraphrased_text:
The adoption of evaluation metrics was initially based on their reported correlations with human judgments when these metrics were first introduced. However, it has since been recognized that the reliability of these correlations can diminish when comparing models of different types or when models become more sophisticated. In the meantime, several new evaluation metrics have been proposed to better align with human judgments, yet the broader research community has been slow to embrace these advancements.

Despite the availability of improved evaluation metrics, recent studies indicate that a significant portion of machine translation and summarization papers from NAACL and ACL 2021 continue to rely solely on traditional metrics like BLEU and ROUGE for model evaluation. In fact, our analysis, consistent with previous metaevaluations, revealed that 68% of machine translation papers and 66% of summarization papers evaluated their models exclusively using BLEU and ROUGE, respectively. Only a small fraction, 5% in the case of machine translation, utilized more contemporary metrics incorporating contextual representations such as COMET.

This highlights a gap between the development of new evaluation metrics and their adoption within the research community. Efforts to encourage the use of these newer, more comprehensive metrics may help advance the field and provide a more accurate assessment of model performance.

title: {A}nswer{S}umm: A Manually-Curated Dataset and Pipeline for Answer Summarization
human_text: We present a sample of the 8 answers to that question on StackExchange and a manually-curated summary covering the answers' main perspectives. Answer summarization is a form of query-based, multi-document summarization (Ernst et al., 2020) , and creating answer summaries that reflect the underlying varying perspectives entails several subtasks: selection of answer sentences relevant to the question (query sentence relevance), grouping these sentences based on perspectives (clustering), summarizing each perspective (cluster summarization), and producing an overall fused summary (fusion). To date, most CQA fora have a notion of a 'best answer,' which is either manually chosen by the person who asked the question or by a moderator, or obtained via community ratings. Work in this field typically makes use of this best answer as a proxy for summaries, i.e. the focus is on extractivelike summaries (Tomasoni and Huang, 2010; Chan et al., 2012; Pande et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2014; Song et al., 2017) . Datasets such as WikiHowQA (Deng et al., 2020a) , which consists of a question, a long answer, and an answer summary, focus on answer selection and the summarization of a single answer.
paraphrased_text: We have gathered 8 answers from StackExchange to a specific question and created a summary that covers the main perspectives of these answers. Answer summarization involves selecting relevant sentences, grouping them based on perspectives, summarizing each perspective, and creating an overall summary. In most question-and-answer forums, a 'best answer' is selected manually or through community ratings. Existing work in this area focuses on extractive summaries based on the best answer. Some datasets like WikiHowQA focus on selecting answers and summarizing individual answers.

title: Locally Aggregated Feature Attribution on Natural Language Model Understanding
human_text: One is model agnostic approaches including Shapley regression values (Shapley, 1953) and LIME (Ribeiro et al., 2016) . We can apply these methods regardless of the model structure, however, they could suffer from computational inefficiency in the scenario of high dimensional input space and complex deep models when making inferences across all possible permutations or with small perturbations in the local neighborhood. Another direction is model-specific approaches which look into the internal model mechanism to understand specific models. Gradient-based feature attribution models are often adopted to explain neural networks since gradients can be easily accessed through back-propagation, which gives a great computational advantage over model-agnostic methods. Since the gradient map itself is often noisy and challenging to interpret, most gradient-based methods aim to stabilize the feature attribution score by smoothing the gradients or learning from the reference data (Sundararajan et al., 2017; Smilkov et al., 2017; Lundberg and Lee, 2017) .
paraphrased_text:
Two types of interpretability approaches are commonly used in the field of machine learning. The first type, known as model-agnostic approaches, includes Shapley regression values (Shapley, 1953) and LIME (Ribeiro et al., 2016). These methods are applicable across various model structures; however, they may encounter computational inefficiencies when dealing with high-dimensional input spaces and complex deep models. This is especially evident when considering the challenge of inferring insights from all potential permutations or when working with subtle perturbations within a local neighborhood.

Conversely, the second type of approach involves model-specific methods that delve into the internal mechanisms of individual models to gain a more targeted understanding. For instance, gradient-based feature attribution models are commonly used to elucidate the inner workings of neural networks. Leveraging back-propagation to access gradients provides these methods with a computational advantage over model-agnostic techniques. Nevertheless, the inherent noise and complexity of gradient maps often pose challenges in interpretation. To address this, many gradient-based approaches focus on stabilizing feature attribution scores through techniques such as gradient smoothing or learning from reference data (Sundararajan et al., 2017; Smilkov et al., 2017; Lundberg and Lee, 2017).

title: {S}em{E}val-2022 Task 11: Multilingual Complex Named Entity Recognition ({M}ulti{C}o{NER})
human_text: Ambiguous Entities and Contexts Particularly for voice and search domains: Some NEs are ambiguous: they are not always entities, e.g. "Inside Out", "Among Us", and "Bonanza" may refer to NEs (a movie, video game, and TV show) in some contexts, but not in others. Such NEs often resemble regular syntactic constituents. News texts have long sentences discussing many entities, but other use cases (search queries, questions) have shorter inputs. Data with minimal context is needed to assess performance of such use cases. Capitalization/punctuation features are large drivers of success in NER (Mayhew et al., 2019) , but short inputs (ASR, queries) often lack such surface features.
paraphrased_text:
Ambiguity in Named Entities and Contexts, Particularly in Voice and Search Domains:

Certain named entities (NEs) exhibit ambiguity by not always representing concrete entities. For example, terms like "Inside Out," "Among Us," and "Bonanza" may refer to specific NEs, such as a movie, video game, or TV show, in certain contexts but not in others. These NEs can sometimes be mistaken for regular syntactic constituents. While news texts tend to feature long sentences that discuss multiple entities, other scenarios, such as search queries or questions, involve shorter inputs. Evaluating the performance of systems in these contexts requires data with minimal context. Notably, capitalization and punctuation features play a significant role in the success of Named Entity Recognition (NER) systems (Mayhew et al., 2019). However, short inputs like automatic speech recognition (ASR) transcriptions and search queries often lack these surface-level features, posing a challenge for accurate entity identification in such cases.

title: Tracking Textual Similarities in Neo-Latin Drama Networks
human_text: Related Work: In the last decade, several tools have been made available for tracking proper text reuse through text alignment or feature extraction for historical languages. The most well-known of these tools (TRACER, foot_2 Tesserae, foot_3 and Passim foot_4 ) have also been tested for Latin: one of the last experiments is that of Franzini, Passarotti, Moritz and Büchler (2018), in which a thorough exploration of HTRD (Historical Text Reuse Detection) tools can be found. These tools can be quite powerful in detecting precise reuse, both intentional and unintentional, in the forms of quoting and allusion. While more traditional HTRD methods will be employed in the future, we wanted to explore the possibilities of the application of older approaches (Cosine Similarity and Stylometry) to a novel case study, shifting towards a more general textual similarity framework that will serve as a solid base for future inquiries.
paraphrased_text:
In the past decade, several tools have emerged for tracking proper text reuse by aligning text or extracting features in historical languages. Among the most well-known tools are TRACER, Tesserae, and Passim, which have also been tested for Latin. One notable experiment was conducted by Franzini, Passarotti, Moritz, and Büchler in 2018, providing a comprehensive exploration of Historical Text Reuse Detection (HTRD) tools. These tools are effective in detecting precise reuse, whether intentional or unintentional, including quoting and allusion.

While traditional HTRD methods will continue to be utilized, we sought to investigate the potential application of older techniques such as Cosine Similarity and Stylometry in a new case study. This shift towards a more general textual similarity framework aims to establish a strong foundation for future research endeavors.
