title: Legal Named Entity Recognition with Multi-Task Domain Adaptation
human_text: We summarize the contributions as follows: • We propose a multi-task domain adversarial model that is jointly trained on two domains, namely general and legal; • We evaluate the performances of our approach, the quality of the predictions, and propose a way of visualizing the embedding space of the named entities; • To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to experiment with domain adaptation in the legal NER. Related Work Named Entity Recognition. In the supervised setting, Lample et al. (2016) introduced LSTMbased neural architectures that do not rely on domain-specific resources or hand-crafted features. Both character-level and word-level representations passed through LSTM and CRF layers proved effective in NER.
paraphrased_text:
Related Work: Named Entity Recognition has seen advancements in recent years. In the supervised setting, Lample et al. (2016) introduced LSTM-based neural architectures that do not rely on domain-specific resources or hand-crafted features. Their approach, utilizing both character-level and word-level representations passed through LSTM and CRF layers, proved to be effective in NER. 

Our contributions in this study are summarized as follows: We propose a multi-task domain adversarial model that is jointly trained on two domains, general and legal. Through our approach, we evaluate the performances, the quality of predictions, and also introduce a method for visualizing the embedding space of named entities. Notably, our work marks the first exploration of domain adaptation in legal NER to the best of our knowledge.

title: Improving Sharpness-Aware Minimization with Fisher Mask for Better Generalization on Language Models
human_text: While fine-tuning the entire PLM can improve performance on a wide range of NLP tasks, it usually suffers from overfitting and poorer generalization ability (Xu et al., 2021; Bahri et al., 2022) , especially in the largescale PLMs and limited training data scenarios. Hence, some existing efforts attempt to provide more regularization in the fine-tuning stage (Zhang et al., 2018; Müller et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2021) , among which the optimization of the training loss is an intuitive and effective method. Specifically, motivated by the finding (Keskar et al., 2016; Neyshabur et al., 2017) that the smoother loss landscape refers to the better model generalization, Foret et al. (2020) propose the "sharpness-aware minimization" (SAM) to simultaneously minimize loss value and loss sharpness, where the sharpness can be quantified as the maximized difference of loss when a perturbation is added to the current weights.
paraphrased_text:
Improving the overall performance of a pre-trained language model (PLM) through fine-tuning can enhance its effectiveness across various natural language processing (NLP) tasks. However, this process often faces challenges such as overfitting and reduced generalization capabilities, especially when dealing with large-scale PLMs and limited training data scenarios (Xu et al., 2021; Bahri et al., 2022). To address these issues, researchers have explored methods to introduce more regularization during the fine-tuning phase (Zhang et al., 2018; Müller et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2021). One intuitive and effective approach is to optimize the training loss.

Drawing inspiration from studies (Keskar et al., 2016; Neyshabur et al., 2017) highlighting that a smoother loss landscape tends to result in better model generalization, Foret et al. (2020) introduced "sharpness-aware minimization" (SAM). SAM aims to minimize both the loss value and loss sharpness simultaneously. Here, sharpness is defined as the maximum difference in loss when a perturbation is applied to the current weights, providing a quantifiable measure of loss landscape characteristics.

title: Towards Realistic Low-resource Relation Extraction: A Benchmark with Empirical Baseline Study
human_text: To shed light on future research on low-resource RE, our empirical analysis suggests that: (i) Previous state-of-the-art methods in the low-resource setting still struggle to obtain better performance than that in the fully-supervised setting (Cross-sentence LRE is extremely challenging), which indicates that there is still much room for low-resource RE. (ii) Balancing methods may not always benefit low-resource RE. The long-tailed issue can not be ignored, and more studies should be focused on model development. (iii) With some simple data augmentation methods, better performance can be achieved, highlighting opportunities for future improvements on low-resource RE. 2 Background on Low-resource RE 2.1 Low-resource RE RE is a classification task that aims to assign relation labels to entity pairs in given contexts.
paraphrased_text:
To provide insights for future research on low-resource Relation Extraction (RE), our empirical analysis reveals the following key points: 

1. Despite advancements in state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior performance in low-resource settings remains a challenge compared to fully-supervised settings. This indicates significant potential for improvement in low-resource RE, particularly in complex cross-sentence scenarios. 

2. While balancing methods may not always enhance low-resource RE outcomes, it is crucial to address the long-tailed issue and prioritize further research on model development. 

3. By implementing straightforward data augmentation techniques, notable performance enhancements can be attained, underscoring promising avenues for advancing low-resource RE. 

Background on Low-resource RE: 
Low-resource RE involves the classification task of assigning relation labels to entity pairs within specified contexts.

title: x-enVENT: A Corpus of Event Descriptions with Experiencer-specific Emotion and Appraisal Annotations
human_text: Hence, (1) we mark the textual spans corresponding to the emotion-triggering events and all of their experiencers (the writer being a special token), (2) we draw relations between the two, and (3) we specify what emotion results from their interplay. An example is shown in Figure 1 , where the writer feels guilty for their own behaviour, while the person affected by it more likely feels sadness. These ⟨event, experiencer, emotion⟩ tuples reflect the model of appraisal (Scherer, 1989) , according to which emotions arise in response to the cognitive evaluation of events. This psychological theory places emotions in a multidimensional space: each dimension represents a specific property of the event being evaluated, for instance its perceived pleasantness (likely to be high with joy and low with disgust), the mental or physical effort that it can be expected to cause in the experiencer (likely high with episodes of anger or fear), the responsibility held by the experiencer for what has happened (high for guilt) (Smith and Ellsworth, 1985) . The annotation unit that we present in this work therefore consists of ⟨ev, exp, emo, -----→ appraisal⟩ tuples, in which the event (ev) and the experiencer (exp) are token spans, the emotion (emo) is a single emotion label, and -----→ appraisal is a vector of numeric values for various appraisal dimensions.
paraphrased_text:
Therefore, our approach involves three main steps: 
1. Identifying the relevant parts of the text related to events that trigger emotions and the individuals involved, including the writer as a special case.
2. Establishing connections between these events and individuals.
3. Describing the resulting emotions from these interactions. 

For instance, in Figure 1, the writer experiences guilt for their actions, while the affected person is more likely to feel sadness. These sets of information (event, individual, emotion) are based on the appraisal model (Scherer, 1989), which suggests that emotions stem from the cognitive assessment of events. This theory views emotions as existing in a multidimensional space, where each dimension represents different aspects of the evaluated event, such as perceived pleasantness (joy vs. disgust), the expected mental or physical effort on the individual (e.g., anger or fear), and the responsibility attributed to the individual (e.g., guilt) (Smith and Ellsworth, 1985). In our work, the annotation unit comprises sets of (event, individual, emotion, appraisal) data, with the event and individual represented by token spans, the emotion by a single label, and the appraisal by a vector of numerical values for different appraisal dimensions.

title: Does Simultaneous Speech Translation need Simultaneous Models?
human_text: This results in high computational costs that contrast with rising awareness on the need to reduce energy consumption (Strubell et al., 2019) towards more sustainable AI (Vinuesa et al., 2020; Schwartz et al., 2020) . So far, the benefits of training systems on partial inputs have been taken for granted and, although works employing models trained in offline mode are documented in literature (Nguyen et al., 2021; Ma et al., 2021) , the indispensability of simultaneous training in SimulST has never been demonstrated. With an eye at the burden and environmental impact of training multiple dedicated models for different tasks -offline, simultaneous -and latency regimes, in this work we address the following question: Does simultaneous speech translation actually need models trained in simultaneous mode? To this end, we experiment with a single, easy-tomaintain offline model, which can effectively serve both the simultaneous and offline tasks. Specifically, we explore the application of the widely adopted wait-k policy to the offline-trained ST system only at inference time, bypassing any additional training neither to adapt the model to the simultaneous scenario nor to accommodate different latency requirements.
paraphrased_text:
This leads to high computational costs, which conflict with the increasing focus on reducing energy consumption in the move towards more sustainable AI. While previous research has acknowledged the advantages of training systems on partial inputs, the necessity of simultaneous training in SimulST has yet to be proven. Although some studies have used models trained in offline mode, the essential nature of simultaneous training remains unestablished. 

Considering the burden and environmental impact of training separate models for different tasks and latency conditions, this study aims to answer the question: Is training models in simultaneous mode necessary for simultaneous speech translation? The experiment involves using a single, easy-to-maintain offline model that can effectively handle both simultaneous and offline tasks. By implementing the wait-k policy at inference time only, without additional training to adapt the model for simultaneous scenarios or different latency requirements, we explore the model's performance in this context.

title: SQUIRE: A Sequence-to-sequence Framework for Multi-hop Knowledge Graph Reasoning
human_text: Moreover, on two sparse KGs, our model outperforms DacKGR (Lv et al., 2020) , which is specifically designed to handle the sparse setting. SQUIRE takes 4x-7x less time to converge on larger KG datasets while obtaining better performance. To the best of our knowledge, SQUIRE is the first sequence-to-sequence framework for multihop reasoning, and may provide a new paradigm for future studies. Related Work: Knowledge Graph Embedding Knowledge graph embedding (KGE) methods map entities to vectors in low-dimensional embedding space, and model relations as transformations between entity embeddings. Each of these models is equipped with a scoring function that maps any triple to a scalar score, which measures the likelihood of the triple.
paraphrased_text:
Furthermore, our model surpasses DacKGR (Lv et al., 2020) on two sparse KGs, despite DacKGR being specifically tailored for sparse settings. SQUIRE exhibits faster convergence times, ranging from 4 to 7 times quicker on larger KG datasets, while achieving superior performance. To our knowledge, SQUIRE stands as the pioneering sequence-to-sequence framework for multihop reasoning, potentially introducing a novel paradigm for future research endeavors.

Related Work
Knowledge Graph Embedding
Knowledge graph embedding (KGE) techniques involve the mapping of entities to vectors within low-dimensional embedding spaces and representing relations as transformations between entity embeddings. Each model in this domain incorporates a scoring function that assigns a scalar score to any given triple, indicating the likelihood of that particular triple.

title: Astro-mT5: Entity Extraction from Astrophysics Literature using mT5 Language Model
human_text: Adding token-level classifiers or CRFs above the sentence encoders is a popular strategy for NER task (Chiu and Nichols, 2015; Strubell et al., 2017; Ma and Hovy, 2016) . (2014) empirically showed that using pre-trained word embeddings such as GloVe along with CNNbased (Ma and Hovy, 2016) and LSTM-based models (Lample et al., 2016) produced better results on the NER task. Recent releases of transformer based PLMs such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) , SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019) , T5 (Raffel et al., 2019) , RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019) , Big-BIRD (Zaheer et al., 2020) , ALBERT (Lan et al., 2019) , RemBERT (Chung et al., 2020) , and Longformer (Beltagy et al., 2020) showed a significant performance improvement in many downstream tasks in NLP such as NER and RE. Additionally, Akbik et al. (2018) developed an easy-to-use interface namely FLAIR, that allows users to fine-tune any word embedding and any PLMs to produce improved results on the NER task.
paraphrased_text:
In the realm of Named Entity Recognition (NER) tasks, a common approach involves incorporating token-level classifiers or Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) above the sentence encoders. Studies by Chiu and Nichols (2015), Strubell et al. (2017), and Ma and Hovy (2016) have highlighted the effectiveness of this strategy. In a related study, (2014) demonstrated the enhanced performance achieved by utilizing pre-trained word embeddings like GloVe in conjunction with CNN-based models (Ma and Hovy, 2016) and LSTM-based models (Lample et al., 2016) for NER tasks.

The introduction of transformer-based Pre-trained Language Models (PLMs) such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019), T5 (Raffel et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), Big-BIRD (Zaheer et al., 2020), ALBERT (Lan et al., 2019), RemBERT (Chung et al., 2020), and Longformer (Beltagy et al., 2020) has significantly elevated performance levels in various Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks, including NER and Relation Extraction (RE).

Furthermore, Akbik et al. (2018) have introduced FLAIR, an intuitive interface that facilitates the fine-tuning of word embeddings and PLMs. This tool empowers users to optimize their models effortlessly and achieve superior results in NER tasks.

title: Continual Learning of Neural Machine Translation within Low Forgetting Risk Regions
human_text: The first method is to find out which parameters have the least impact on the previous task loss. We use the curvature of the loss curve as an indicator and the parameter with small curvature indicates that the loss curve is relatively flat, so the parameters can be updated more greedily. The second method is based on the impacts of parameters on the model output of the previous task. We define an objective function, which tries to maximize the size of update regions as much as possible while restricting the change of model output on the previous task, to help the model learn the update regions automatically. We conduct experiments on the domain adaptation and more challenging language adaptation tasks, and the results show that our method can achieve significant improvements compared with several strong baselines.
paraphrased_text:
To improve the overall efficiency of parameter updating, two methods can be employed. The first method involves identifying parameters that have the least impact on the loss of the previous task. This is determined by analyzing the curvature of the loss curve, with smaller curvature indicating a flatter loss curve and allowing for more aggressive parameter updates. 

The second method focuses on assessing the impacts of parameters on the model output of the previous task. An objective function is defined to maximize the size of update regions while constraining changes to the model output of the previous task. This approach helps the model automatically learn the update regions. 

Experimental evaluations were conducted on domain adaptation and challenging language adaptation tasks. The results demonstrate that our method outperforms several strong baselines, leading to significant improvements in performance.
